revision.text:
{{Shrine | name = Uonuma Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Uonuma Shrine.JPG|270px]]
Main Hall | location = [[Niigata Prefecture]][[Ojiya, Niigata|Ojiya]] Tsuchikawa 2-chōme 699-1 | coordinates = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} | Enshrined deities = [[Amenokaguyama-no-Mikoto]] | Shrine rank = [[Shikinaisha]] (disputed), Traditional Second Shrine of [[Echigo Province]], Former [[Prefectural Shrine]] | consecration_year = Traditionally during the reign of [[Emperor Sujin]] | Main building = [[Nagare-zukuri]] | Alternate names = Kami-Yahiko Daimyōjin | Pilgrimage sites = | festival = [[September 7]] | Rituals = | Map = {{location map|Japan Niigata|label=Uonuma Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Uonuma Shrine''' (魚沼神社, ''Uonuma Jinja'') is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Ojiya, Niigata|Ojiya]], [[Niigata Prefecture]]. The shrine grounds include the nationally designated Important Cultural Property, the Amida Hall. == Enshrined deities == * [[Ame-no-Kaguyama]] * [[Toyotama-hime-no-Mikoto]] * Ahi-no-kami (Ahiko) == History == The shrine's foundation is traditionally attributed to the reign of [[Emperor Sujin]]. The name "Uonuma Shrine" appears in documents from the [[Heian period]] and is recorded in the ''[[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]'' among the five shrines in [[Uonuma District]]. However, its identification as the same Uonuma Shrine listed in the ''Engishiki'' has been debated since the [[Edo period]]. During the [[Sengoku period (Japan)|Sengoku period]], due to its shared deity with [[Yahiko Shrine]] in [[Yahiko, Niigata|Yahiko]], it was referred to as "Kami-Yahiko Shrine." Historical documents from the era of [[Uesugi Kenshin]] mention it as "Kami-Yahiko Daimyōjin," and a plaque reading "Yahiko Daimyōjin" is displayed in the worship hall. The shrine served as a religious center for the region and was later called "Uonuma Shrine" during the [[Bakumatsu]] period. It was relocated to its current location in Tsuchikawa from the earlier site at Kaminoyama. Key historical milestones include: * [[Jōgan 3]] (861 CE): Uonuma Shrine received the rank of [[Junior Fifth Rank|Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade]]. * [[Genkō 3]] (1333 CE): Shrine priests supported [[Nitta Yoshisada]] during his uprising. * [[Eikyō 9]] (1437 CE): The shrine received a large gong donated by Saikata Yasaburō Mitsuyuki (now a Niigata Prefecture-designated cultural property). * [[Eiroku 5]] (1562 CE): [[Uesugi Kenshin]] donated shrine lands and offerings for 18 branch shrines. * [[Eiroku 12]] (1569 CE): A hand-copied version of the ''[[Daihannya-kyō]]'' was donated to the shrine. * [[Meiji 6]] (1873 CE): Designated as a [[Gōsha]] and later promoted to [[Prefectural Shrine]]. == Shrine grounds == The main shrine gate is a [[Ryōbu torii]]. Adjacent to the purification fountain, stone steps lead to a historical water purification site. The shrine grounds include the Amida Hall to the left, the Kagura Hall to the right, and a monument commemorating the Russo-Japanese War nearby. ; Amida Hall The Amida Hall, constructed in [[1563 CE]] during the [[Muromachi period]], is designated as a National Important Cultural Property. It is a modest hōgyō-zukuri (hipped roof) structure with thatched roofing. Inside are statues of [[Amida Nyorai]] and [[Dainichi Nyorai]], said to have been crafted from iron sand sourced from the [[Shinano River]]. The hall was restored in [[1954 CE]] to its original form. == Cultural Properties == === National Important Cultural Properties === * Amida Hall - Designated under the former Ancient Shrines and Temples Preservation Act in [[1906 CE]]. === Niigata Prefecture-Designated Cultural Properties === * Large Gong - Crafted in [[1437 CE]] with inscriptions by Saikata Yasaburō Mitsuyuki. * ''Daihannya-kyō'' (559 scrolls) - Donated in [[1569 CE]]. === Ojiya City-Designated Cultural Properties === * Mikoshi - A portable shrine featuring a phoenix on its roof, originally crafted in [[1495 CE]] and restored in [[1817 CE]]. * ''Uonuma Shrine Annual Event Record'' - Historical documents outlining shrine activities. == Legends == === Uesugi Kenshin and the Divine Army === Uesugi Kenshin is said to have visited the shrine to pray for victory before crossing the [[Shinano River]] en route to the [[Kantō region]]. During one such visit, he reportedly saw a vision of [[Ame-no-Kaguyama]] and other deities marching toward Kantō. The vision inspired his troops and contributed to their subsequent victories. === The Rescued Soldier === During [[World War II]], a soldier who had fallen into a dry well was rescued by a white-haired elderly man holding a lantern marked with "Kami-Yahiko Shrine." Upon being reunited with his unit, the elderly man disappeared. === The Giant Cedar === A massive cedar tree known as "Ōki-sama" stood on the shrine grounds until it was toppled by a storm. Its trunk reportedly stretched down the mountain, serving as a makeshift bridge. == References == * Igarashi Shūtarō, ''Legends of Ojiya,'' Kōbunsha, 1985. * Ojiya City Historical Committee, ''Chronicle of Ojiya,'' Ojiya City Cultural Property Association, 1984. * Ojiya City Board of Education, ''History of Ojiya,'' 2007. == External links == * [http://www.city.ojiya.niigata.jp/soshiki/shogai/uonumajinja-amidado.html Uonuma Shrine Amida Hall] - Ojiya City Official Website * [https://niigata-kankou.or.jp/spot/6089 Niigata Tourism Guide: Uonuma Shrine Amida Hall] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Niigata Prefecture]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha]] [[Category:Syncretic shrines]] [[Category:Muromachi period architecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Cultural Properties of Niigata Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Ojiya, Niigata]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Ojiya, Niigata]] [[Category:魚沼神社]] [[Category:Default sort converted to citation needed]] {{citation needed|Uonuma Shrine}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Uonuma Shrine]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[Category:Default sort converted to citation needed]] {{citation needed|Uonuma Shrine}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Uonuma Shrine]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] {{citation needed|Uonuma Shrine}}[[Category:refreshing the redirects]] [[Category:mass bump]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[Category:bump 1-25]] [[ja:魚沼神社]]

Its trunk reportedly stretched down the mountain, serving as a makeshift bridge. == References == * Igarashi Shūtarō, ''Legends of Ojiya,'' Kōbunsha, 1985. * Ojiya City Historical Committee, ''Chronicle of Ojiya,'' Ojiya City Cultural Property Association, 1984. * Ojiya City Board of Education, ''History of Ojiya,'' 2007. == External links == * [http://www.city.ojiya.niigata.jp/soshiki/shogai/uonumajinja-amidado.html Uonuma Shrine Amida Hall] - Ojiya City Official Website * [https://niigata-kankou.or.jp/spot/6089 Niigata Tourism Guide: Uonuma Shrine Amida Hall] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Niigata Prefecture]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha]] [[Category:Syncretic shrines]] [[Category:Muromachi period architecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Cultural Properties of Niigata Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Ojiya, Niigata]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Ojiya, Niigata]] [[Category:魚沼神社]] [[Category:Default sort converted to citation needed]] {{citation needed|Uonuma Shrine}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Uonuma Shrine]][[Category:Default sort converted to citation needed]] {{citation needed|Uonuma Shrine}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Uonuma Shrine]]{{citation needed|Uonuma Shrine}}[[Category:refreshing the redirects]] [[Category:mass bump]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[Category:bump 1-25]] [[Category:nnn]] [[ja:魚沼神社]]

revision.text:
{{Shrine | name = Uonuma Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Uonuma Shrine.JPG|270px]]
Main Hall | location = [[Niigata Prefecture]][[Ojiya, Niigata|Ojiya]] Tsuchikawa 2-chōme 699-1 | coordinates = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} | Enshrined deities = [[Amenokaguyama-no-Mikoto]] | Shrine rank = [[Shikinaisha]] (disputed), Traditional Second Shrine of [[Echigo Province]], Former [[Prefectural Shrine]] | consecration_year = Traditionally during the reign of [[Emperor Sujin]] | Main building = [[Nagare-zukuri]] | Alternate names = Kami-Yahiko Daimyōjin | Pilgrimage sites = | festival = [[September 7]] | Rituals = | Map = {{location map|Japan Niigata|label=Uonuma Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Uonuma Shrine''' (魚沼神社, ''Uonuma Jinja'') is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Ojiya, Niigata|Ojiya]], [[Niigata Prefecture]]. The shrine grounds include the nationally designated Important Cultural Property, the Amida Hall. == Enshrined deities == * [[Ame-no-Kaguyama]] * [[Toyotama-hime-no-Mikoto]] * Ahi-no-kami (Ahiko) == History == The shrine's foundation is traditionally attributed to the reign of [[Emperor Sujin]]. The name "Uonuma Shrine" appears in documents from the [[Heian period]] and is recorded in the ''[[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]'' among the five shrines in [[Uonuma District]]. However, its identification as the same Uonuma Shrine listed in the ''Engishiki'' has been debated since the [[Edo period]]. During the [[Sengoku period (Japan)|Sengoku period]], due to its shared deity with [[Yahiko Shrine]] in [[Yahiko, Niigata|Yahiko]], it was referred to as "Kami-Yahiko Shrine." Historical documents from the era of [[Uesugi Kenshin]] mention it as "Kami-Yahiko Daimyōjin," and a plaque reading "Yahiko Daimyōjin" is displayed in the worship hall. The shrine served as a religious center for the region and was later called "Uonuma Shrine" during the [[Bakumatsu]] period. It was relocated to its current location in Tsuchikawa from the earlier site at Kaminoyama. Key historical milestones include: * [[Jōgan 3]] (861 CE): Uonuma Shrine received the rank of [[Junior Fifth Rank|Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade]]. * [[Genkō 3]] (1333 CE): Shrine priests supported [[Nitta Yoshisada]] during his uprising. * [[Eikyō 9]] (1437 CE): The shrine received a large gong donated by Saikata Yasaburō Mitsuyuki (now a Niigata Prefecture-designated cultural property). * [[Eiroku 5]] (1562 CE): [[Uesugi Kenshin]] donated shrine lands and offerings for 18 branch shrines. * [[Eiroku 12]] (1569 CE): A hand-copied version of the ''[[Daihannya-kyō]]'' was donated to the shrine. * [[Meiji 6]] (1873 CE): Designated as a [[Gōsha]] and later promoted to [[Prefectural Shrine]]. == Shrine grounds == The main shrine gate is a [[Ryōbu torii]]. Adjacent to the purification fountain, stone steps lead to a historical water purification site. The shrine grounds include the Amida Hall to the left, the Kagura Hall to the right, and a monument commemorating the Russo-Japanese War nearby. ; Amida Hall The Amida Hall, constructed in [[1563 CE]] during the [[Muromachi period]], is designated as a National Important Cultural Property. It is a modest hōgyō-zukuri (hipped roof) structure with thatched roofing. Inside are statues of [[Amida Nyorai]] and [[Dainichi Nyorai]], said to have been crafted from iron sand sourced from the [[Shinano River]]. The hall was restored in [[1954 CE]] to its original form. == Cultural Properties == === National Important Cultural Properties === * Amida Hall - Designated under the former Ancient Shrines and Temples Preservation Act in [[1906 CE]]. === Niigata Prefecture-Designated Cultural Properties === * Large Gong - Crafted in [[1437 CE]] with inscriptions by Saikata Yasaburō Mitsuyuki. * ''Daihannya-kyō'' (559 scrolls) - Donated in [[1569 CE]]. === Ojiya City-Designated Cultural Properties === * Mikoshi - A portable shrine featuring a phoenix on its roof, originally crafted in [[1495 CE]] and restored in [[1817 CE]]. * ''Uonuma Shrine Annual Event Record'' - Historical documents outlining shrine activities. == Legends == === Uesugi Kenshin and the Divine Army === Uesugi Kenshin is said to have visited the shrine to pray for victory before crossing the [[Shinano River]] en route to the [[Kantō region]]. During one such visit, he reportedly saw a vision of [[Ame-no-Kaguyama]] and other deities marching toward Kantō. The vision inspired his troops and contributed to their subsequent victories. === The Rescued Soldier === During [[World War II]], a soldier who had fallen into a dry well was rescued by a white-haired elderly man holding a lantern marked with "Kami-Yahiko Shrine." Upon being reunited with his unit, the elderly man disappeared. === The Giant Cedar === A massive cedar tree known as "Ōki-sama" stood on the shrine grounds until it was toppled by a storm. Its trunk reportedly stretched down the mountain, serving as a makeshift bridge. == References == * Igarashi Shūtarō, ''Legends of Ojiya,'' Kōbunsha, 1985. * Ojiya City Historical Committee, ''Chronicle of Ojiya,'' Ojiya City Cultural Property Association, 1984. * Ojiya City Board of Education, ''History of Ojiya,'' 2007. == External links == * [http://www.city.ojiya.niigata.jp/soshiki/shogai/uonumajinja-amidado.html Uonuma Shrine Amida Hall] - Ojiya City Official Website * [https://niigata-kankou.or.jp/spot/6089 Niigata Tourism Guide: Uonuma Shrine Amida Hall] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Niigata Prefecture]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha]] [[Category:Syncretic shrines]] [[Category:Muromachi period architecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Cultural Properties of Niigata Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Ojiya, Niigata]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Ojiya, Niigata]] [[Category:魚沼神社]] [[Category:Default sort converted to citation needed]] {{citation needed|Uonuma Shrine}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-Uonuma Shrine-qq]] [[Category:Uonuma Shrine]][[Category:Default sort converted to citation needed]] {{citation needed|Uonuma Shrine}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-Uonuma Shrine-qq]] [[Category:Uonuma Shrine]]{{citation needed|Uonuma Shrine}}[[Category:refreshing the redirects]] [[Category:mass bump]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[Category:bump 1-25]] [[Category:nnn]] [[ja:魚沼神社]]

Its trunk reportedly stretched down the mountain, serving as a makeshift bridge. == References == * Igarashi Shūtarō, ''Legends of Ojiya,'' Kōbunsha, 1985. * Ojiya City Historical Committee, ''Chronicle of Ojiya,'' Ojiya City Cultural Property Association, 1984. * Ojiya City Board of Education, ''History of Ojiya,'' 2007. == External links == * [http://www.city.ojiya.niigata.jp/soshiki/shogai/uonumajinja-amidado.html Uonuma Shrine Amida Hall] - Ojiya City Official Website * [https://niigata-kankou.or.jp/spot/6089 Niigata Tourism Guide: Uonuma Shrine Amida Hall] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Niigata Prefecture]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha]] [[Category:Syncretic shrines]] [[Category:Muromachi period architecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Cultural Properties of Niigata Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Ojiya, Niigata]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Ojiya, Niigata]] [[Category:魚沼神社]] [[Category:Default sort converted to citation needed]] {{citation needed|Uonuma Shrine}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-{{subst:MAINSPACE}}--qq]] [[Category:qq-Uonuma Shrine-qq]] [[Category:Uonuma Shrine]][[Category:Default sort converted to citation needed]] {{citation needed|Uonuma Shrine}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-{{subst:MAINSPACE}}--qq]] [[Category:qq-Uonuma Shrine-qq]] [[Category:Uonuma Shrine]]{{citation needed|Uonuma Shrine}}[[Category:refreshing the redirects]] [[Category:mass bump]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[Category:bump 1-25]] [[Category:nnn]] [[Category:Subst:MAINSPACE]] [[Category:q--q]] [[ja:魚沼神社]]

{{Shrine | name = Uonuma Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Uonuma Shrine.JPG|270px]]
Main Hall | location = [[Niigata Prefecture]][[Ojiya, Niigata|Ojiya]] Tsuchikawa 2-chōme 699-1 | coordinates = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} | Enshrined deities = [[Amenokaguyama-no-Mikoto]] | Shrine rank = [[Shikinaisha]] (disputed), Traditional Second Shrine of [[Echigo Province]], Former [[Prefectural Shrine]] | consecration_year = Traditionally during the reign of [[Emperor Sujin]] | Main building = [[Nagare-zukuri]] | Alternate names = Kami-Yahiko Daimyōjin | Pilgrimage sites = | festival = [[September 7]] | Rituals = | Map = {{location map|Japan Niigata|label=Uonuma Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Uonuma Shrine''' (魚沼神社, ''Uonuma Jinja'') is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Ojiya, Niigata|Ojiya]], [[Niigata Prefecture]]. The shrine grounds include the nationally designated Important Cultural Property, the Amida Hall. == Enshrined deities == * [[Ame-no-Kaguyama]] * [[Toyotama-hime-no-Mikoto]] * Ahi-no-kami (Ahiko) == History == The shrine's foundation is traditionally attributed to the reign of [[Emperor Sujin]]. The name "Uonuma Shrine" appears in documents from the [[Heian period]] and is recorded in the ''[[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]'' among the five shrines in [[Uonuma District]]. However, its identification as the same Uonuma Shrine listed in the ''Engishiki'' has been debated since the [[Edo period]]. During the [[Sengoku period (Japan)|Sengoku period]], due to its shared deity with [[Yahiko Shrine]] in [[Yahiko, Niigata|Yahiko]], it was referred to as "Kami-Yahiko Shrine." Historical documents from the era of [[Uesugi Kenshin]] mention it as "Kami-Yahiko Daimyōjin," and a plaque reading "Yahiko Daimyōjin" is displayed in the worship hall. The shrine served as a religious center for the region and was later called "Uonuma Shrine" during the [[Bakumatsu]] period. It was relocated to its current location in Tsuchikawa from the earlier site at Kaminoyama. Key historical milestones include: * [[Jōgan 3]] (861 CE): Uonuma Shrine received the rank of [[Junior Fifth Rank|Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade]]. * [[Genkō 3]] (1333 CE): Shrine priests supported [[Nitta Yoshisada]] during his uprising. * [[Eikyō 9]] (1437 CE): The shrine received a large gong donated by Saikata Yasaburō Mitsuyuki (now a Niigata Prefecture-designated cultural property). * [[Eiroku 5]] (1562 CE): [[Uesugi Kenshin]] donated shrine lands and offerings for 18 branch shrines. * [[Eiroku 12]] (1569 CE): A hand-copied version of the ''[[Daihannya-kyō]]'' was donated to the shrine. * [[Meiji 6]] (1873 CE): Designated as a [[Gōsha]] and later promoted to [[Prefectural Shrine]]. == Shrine grounds == The main shrine gate is a [[Ryōbu torii]]. Adjacent to the purification fountain, stone steps lead to a historical water purification site. The shrine grounds include the Amida Hall to the left, the Kagura Hall to the right, and a monument commemorating the Russo-Japanese War nearby. ; Amida Hall The Amida Hall, constructed in [[1563 CE]] during the [[Muromachi period]], is designated as a National Important Cultural Property. It is a modest hōgyō-zukuri (hipped roof) structure with thatched roofing. Inside are statues of [[Amida Nyorai]] and [[Dainichi Nyorai]], said to have been crafted from iron sand sourced from the [[Shinano River]]. The hall was restored in [[1954 CE]] to its original form. == Cultural Properties == === National Important Cultural Properties === * Amida Hall - Designated under the former Ancient Shrines and Temples Preservation Act in [[1906 CE]]. === Niigata Prefecture-Designated Cultural Properties === * Large Gong - Crafted in [[1437 CE]] with inscriptions by Saikata Yasaburō Mitsuyuki. * ''Daihannya-kyō'' (559 scrolls) - Donated in [[1569 CE]]. === Ojiya City-Designated Cultural Properties === * Mikoshi - A portable shrine featuring a phoenix on its roof, originally crafted in [[1495 CE]] and restored in [[1817 CE]]. * ''Uonuma Shrine Annual Event Record'' - Historical documents outlining shrine activities. == Legends == === Uesugi Kenshin and the Divine Army === Uesugi Kenshin is said to have visited the shrine to pray for victory before crossing the [[Shinano River]] en route to the [[Kantō region]]. During one such visit, he reportedly saw a vision of [[Ame-no-Kaguyama]] and other deities marching toward Kantō. The vision inspired his troops and contributed to their subsequent victories. === The Rescued Soldier === During [[World War II]], a soldier who had fallen into a dry well was rescued by a white-haired elderly man holding a lantern marked with "Kami-Yahiko Shrine." Upon being reunited with his unit, the elderly man disappeared. === The Giant Cedar === A massive cedar tree known as "Ōki-sama" stood on the shrine grounds until it was toppled by a storm. Its trunk reportedly stretched down the mountain, serving as a makeshift bridge. == References == * Igarashi Shūtarō, ''Legends of Ojiya,'' Kōbunsha, 1985. * Ojiya City Historical Committee, ''Chronicle of Ojiya,'' Ojiya City Cultural Property Association, 1984. * Ojiya City Board of Education, ''History of Ojiya,'' 2007. == External links == * [http://www.city.ojiya.niigata.jp/soshiki/shogai/uonumajinja-amidado.html Uonuma Shrine Amida Hall] - Ojiya City Official Website * [https://niigata-kankou.or.jp/spot/6089 Niigata Tourism Guide: Uonuma Shrine Amida Hall] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Niigata Prefecture]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha]] [[Category:Syncretic shrines]] [[Category:Muromachi period architecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Cultural Properties of Niigata Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Ojiya, Niigata]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Ojiya, Niigata]] [[Category:魚沼神社]] [[Category:Default sort converted to citation needed]] {{citation needed|Uonuma Shrine}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Uonuma Shrine-qq]] [[Category:Uonuma Shrine]][[Category:Default sort converted to citation needed]] {{citation needed|Uonuma Shrine}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Uonuma Shrine-qq]] [[Category:Uonuma Shrine]]{{citation needed|Uonuma Shrine}}[[Category:refreshing the redirects]] [[Category:mass bump]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[Category:bump 1-25]] [[Category:nnn]] [[Category:Subst:NAMESPACE]] [[Category:q--q]] [[ja:魚沼神社]]

Its trunk reportedly stretched down the mountain, serving as a makeshift bridge. == References == * Igarashi Shūtarō, ''Legends of Ojiya,'' Kōbunsha, 1985. * Ojiya City Historical Committee, ''Chronicle of Ojiya,'' Ojiya City Cultural Property Association, 1984. * Ojiya City Board of Education, ''History of Ojiya,'' 2007. == External links == * [http://www.city.ojiya.niigata.jp/soshiki/shogai/uonumajinja-amidado.html Uonuma Shrine Amida Hall] - Ojiya City Official Website * [https://niigata-kankou.or.jp/spot/6089 Niigata Tourism Guide: Uonuma Shrine Amida Hall] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Niigata Prefecture]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha]] [[Category:Syncretic shrines]] [[Category:Muromachi period architecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Cultural Properties of Niigata Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Ojiya, Niigata]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Ojiya, Niigata]] [[Category:魚沼神社]] [[Category:Default sort converted to citation needed]] {{citation needed|Uonuma Shrine}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Uonuma Shrine-qq]] [[Category:Uonuma Shrine]][[Category:Default sort converted to citation needed]] {{citation needed|Uonuma Shrine}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Uonuma Shrine-qq]] [[Category:Uonuma Shrine]]{{citation needed|Uonuma Shrine}}[[Category:refreshing the redirects]] [[Category:mass bump]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[Category:bump 1-25]] [[Category:nnn]] [[Category:Subst:NAMESPACE]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:qq]][[en:Uonuma Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[ja:魚沼神社]]

revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Uonuma Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Uonuma Shrine.JPG|270px]]
Main Hall | location = [[Niigata Prefecture]][[Ojiya, Niigata|Ojiya]] Tsuchikawa 2-chōme 699-1 | coordinates = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} | Enshrined deities = [[Amenokaguyama-no-Mikoto]] | Shrine rank = [[Shikinaisha]] (disputed), Traditional Second Shrine of [[Echigo Province]], Former [[Prefectural Shrine]] | consecration_year = Traditionally during the reign of [[Emperor Sujin]] | Main building = [[Nagare-zukuri]] | Alternate names = Kami-Yahiko Daimyōjin | Pilgrimage sites = | festival = [[September 7]] | Rituals = | Map = {{location map|Japan Niigata|label=Uonuma Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Uonuma Shrine''' (魚沼神社, ''Uonuma Jinja'') is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Ojiya, Niigata|Ojiya]], [[Niigata Prefecture]]. The shrine grounds include the nationally designated Important Cultural Property, the Amida Hall. == Enshrined deities == * [[Ame-no-Kaguyama]] * [[Toyotama-hime-no-Mikoto]] * Ahi-no-kami (Ahiko) == History == The shrine's foundation is traditionally attributed to the reign of [[Emperor Sujin]]. The name "Uonuma Shrine" appears in documents from the [[Heian period]] and is recorded in the ''[[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]'' among the five shrines in [[Uonuma District]]. However, its identification as the same Uonuma Shrine listed in the ''Engishiki'' has been debated since the [[Edo period]]. During the [[Sengoku period (Japan)|Sengoku period]], due to its shared deity with [[Yahiko Shrine]] in [[Yahiko, Niigata|Yahiko]], it was referred to as "Kami-Yahiko Shrine." Historical documents from the era of [[Uesugi Kenshin]] mention it as "Kami-Yahiko Daimyōjin," and a plaque reading "Yahiko Daimyōjin" is displayed in the worship hall. The shrine served as a religious center for the region and was later called "Uonuma Shrine" during the [[Bakumatsu]] period. It was relocated to its current location in Tsuchikawa from the earlier site at Kaminoyama. Key historical milestones include: * [[Jōgan 3]] (861 CE): Uonuma Shrine received the rank of [[Junior Fifth Rank|Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade]]. * [[Genkō 3]] (1333 CE): Shrine priests supported [[Nitta Yoshisada]] during his uprising. * [[Eikyō 9]] (1437 CE): The shrine received a large gong donated by Saikata Yasaburō Mitsuyuki (now a Niigata Prefecture-designated cultural property). * [[Eiroku 5]] (1562 CE): [[Uesugi Kenshin]] donated shrine lands and offerings for 18 branch shrines. * [[Eiroku 12]] (1569 CE): A hand-copied version of the ''[[Daihannya-kyō]]'' was donated to the shrine. * [[Meiji 6]] (1873 CE): Designated as a [[Gōsha]] and later promoted to [[Prefectural Shrine]]. == Shrine grounds == The main shrine gate is a [[Ryōbu torii]]. Adjacent to the purification fountain, stone steps lead to a historical water purification site. The shrine grounds include the Amida Hall to the left, the Kagura Hall to the right, and a monument commemorating the Russo-Japanese War nearby. ; Amida Hall The Amida Hall, constructed in [[1563 CE]] during the [[Muromachi period]], is designated as a National Important Cultural Property. It is a modest hōgyō-zukuri (hipped roof) structure with thatched roofing. Inside are statues of [[Amida Nyorai]] and [[Dainichi Nyorai]], said to have been crafted from iron sand sourced from the [[Shinano River]]. The hall was restored in [[1954 CE]] to its original form. == Cultural Properties == === National Important Cultural Properties === * Amida Hall - Designated under the former Ancient Shrines and Temples Preservation Act in [[1906 CE]]. === Niigata Prefecture-Designated Cultural Properties === * Large Gong - Crafted in [[1437 CE]] with inscriptions by Saikata Yasaburō Mitsuyuki. * ''Daihannya-kyō'' (559 scrolls) - Donated in [[1569 CE]]. === Ojiya City-Designated Cultural Properties === * Mikoshi - A portable shrine featuring a phoenix on its roof, originally crafted in [[1495 CE]] and restored in [[1817 CE]]. * ''Uonuma Shrine Annual Event Record'' - Historical documents outlining shrine activities. == Legends == === Uesugi Kenshin and the Divine Army === Uesugi Kenshin is said to have visited the shrine to pray for victory before crossing the [[Shinano River]] en route to the [[Kantō region]]. During one such visit, he reportedly saw a vision of [[Ame-no-Kaguyama]] and other deities marching toward Kantō. The vision inspired his troops and contributed to their subsequent victories. === The Rescued Soldier === During [[World War II]], a soldier who had fallen into a dry well was rescued by a white-haired elderly man holding a lantern marked with "Kami-Yahiko Shrine." Upon being reunited with his unit, the elderly man disappeared. === The Giant Cedar === A massive cedar tree known as "Ōki-sama" stood on the shrine grounds until it was toppled by a storm. Its trunk reportedly stretched down the mountain, serving as a makeshift bridge. == References == * Igarashi Shūtarō, ''Legends of Ojiya,'' Kōbunsha, 1985. * Ojiya City Historical Committee, ''Chronicle of Ojiya,'' Ojiya City Cultural Property Association, 1984. * Ojiya City Board of Education, ''History of Ojiya,'' 2007. == External links == * [http://www.city.ojiya.niigata.jp/soshiki/shogai/uonumajinja-amidado.html Uonuma Shrine Amida Hall] - Ojiya City Official Website * [https://niigata-kankou.or.jp/spot/6089 Niigata Tourism Guide: Uonuma Shrine Amida Hall] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Niigata Prefecture]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha]] [[Category:Syncretic shrines]] [[Category:Muromachi period architecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Cultural Properties of Niigata Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Ojiya, Niigata]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Ojiya, Niigata]] [[Category:魚沼神社]] [[Category:Default sort converted to citation needed]] {{citation needed|Uonuma Shrine}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Uonuma Shrine-qq]] [[Category:Uonuma Shrine]][[Category:Default sort converted to citation needed]] {{citation needed|Uonuma Shrine}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Uonuma Shrine-qq]] [[Category:Uonuma Shrine]]{{citation needed|Uonuma Shrine}}[[Category:refreshing the redirects]] [[Category:mass bump]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[Category:bump 1-25]] [[Category:nnn]] [[Category:Subst:NAMESPACE]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:qq]][[en:Uonuma Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:subst:MAINSPACE]][[sn:Uonuma Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Uonuma Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Uonuma Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Uonuma Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Uonuma Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[ja:魚沼神社]]

However, its identification as the same Uonuma Shrine listed in the ''Engishiki'' has been debated since the [[Edo period]]. During the [[Sengoku period (Japan)|Sengoku period]], due to its shared deity with [[Yahiko Shrine]] in [[Yahiko, Niigata|Yahiko]], it was referred to as "Kami-Yahiko Shrine." Historical documents from the era of [[Uesugi Kenshin]] mention it as "Kami-Yahiko Daimyōjin," and a plaque reading "Yahiko Daimyōjin" is displayed in the worship hall. The shrine served as a religious center for the region and was later called "Uonuma Shrine" during the [[Bakumatsu]] period. It was relocated to its current location in Tsuchikawa from the earlier site at Kaminoyama. Key historical milestones include: * [[Jōgan 3]] (861 CE): Uonuma Shrine received the rank of [[Junior Fifth Rank|Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade]]. * [[Genkō 3]] (1333 CE): Shrine priests supported [[Nitta Yoshisada]] during his uprising. * [[Eikyō 9]] (1437 CE): The shrine received a large gong donated by Saikata Yasaburō Mitsuyuki (now a Niigata Prefecture-designated cultural property). * [[Eiroku 5]] (1562 CE): [[Uesugi Kenshin]] donated shrine lands and offerings for 18 branch shrines. * [[Eiroku 12]] (1569 CE): A hand-copied version of the ''[[Daihannya-kyō]]'' was donated to the shrine. * [[Meiji 6]] (1873 CE): Designated as a [[Gōsha]] and later promoted to [[Prefectural Shrine]]. == Shrine grounds == The main shrine gate is a [[Ryōbu torii]]. Adjacent to the purification fountain, stone steps lead to a historical water purification site. The shrine grounds include the Amida Hall to the left, the Kagura Hall to the right, and a monument commemorating the Russo-Japanese War nearby. ; Amida Hall The Amida Hall, constructed in [[1563 CE]] during the [[Muromachi period]], is designated as a National Important Cultural Property. It is a modest hōgyō-zukuri (hipped roof) structure with thatched roofing. Inside are statues of [[Amida Nyorai]] and [[Dainichi Nyorai]], said to have been crafted from iron sand sourced from the [[Shinano River]]. The hall was restored in [[1954 CE]] to its original form. == Cultural Properties == === National Important Cultural Properties === * Amida Hall - Designated under the former Ancient Shrines and Temples Preservation Act in [[1906 CE]]. === Niigata Prefecture-Designated Cultural Properties === * Large Gong - Crafted in [[1437 CE]] with inscriptions by Saikata Yasaburō Mitsuyuki. * ''Daihannya-kyō'' (559 scrolls) - Donated in [[1569 CE]]. === Ojiya City-Designated Cultural Properties === * Mikoshi - A portable shrine featuring a phoenix on its roof, originally crafted in [[1495 CE]] and restored in [[1817 CE]]. * ''Uonuma Shrine Annual Event Record'' - Historical documents outlining shrine activities. == Legends == === Uesugi Kenshin and the Divine Army === Uesugi Kenshin is said to have visited the shrine to pray for victory before crossing the [[Shinano River]] en route to the [[Kantō region]]. During one such visit, he reportedly saw a vision of [[Ame-no-Kaguyama]] and other deities marching toward Kantō. The vision inspired his troops and contributed to their subsequent victories. === The Rescued Soldier === During [[World War II]], a soldier who had fallen into a dry well was rescued by a white-haired elderly man holding a lantern marked with "Kami-Yahiko Shrine." Upon being reunited with his unit, the elderly man disappeared. === The Giant Cedar === A massive cedar tree known as "Ōki-sama" stood on the shrine grounds until it was toppled by a storm. Its trunk reportedly stretched down the mountain, serving as a makeshift bridge. == References == * Igarashi Shūtarō, ''Legends of Ojiya,'' Kōbunsha, 1985. * Ojiya City Historical Committee, ''Chronicle of Ojiya,'' Ojiya City Cultural Property Association, 1984. * Ojiya City Board of Education, ''History of Ojiya,'' 2007. == External links == * [http://www.city.ojiya.niigata.jp/soshiki/shogai/uonumajinja-amidado.html Uonuma Shrine Amida Hall] - Ojiya City Official Website * [https://niigata-kankou.or.jp/spot/6089 Niigata Tourism Guide: Uonuma Shrine Amida Hall] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Niigata Prefecture]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha]] [[Category:Syncretic shrines]] [[Category:Muromachi period architecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Cultural Properties of Niigata Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Ojiya, Niigata]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Ojiya, Niigata]] [[Category:魚沼神社]] [[Category:Default sort converted to citation needed]] {{citation needed|Uonuma Shrine}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Uonuma Shrine-qq]] [[Category:Uonuma Shrine]][[Category:Default sort converted to citation needed]] {{citation needed|Uonuma Shrine}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Uonuma Shrine-qq]] [[Category:Uonuma Shrine]]{{citation needed|Uonuma Shrine}}[[Category:refreshing the redirects]] [[Category:mass bump]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[Category:bump 1-25]] [[Category:nnn]] [[Category:Subst:NAMESPACE]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:qq]][[en:Uonuma Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:subst:MAINSPACE]][[sn:Uonuma Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Uonuma Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Uonuma Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Uonuma Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Uonuma Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:subst:MAINSPACE]][[sn:Uonuma Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Uonuma Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Uonuma Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Uonuma Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:subst:MAINSPACE]][[sn:Uonuma Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Uonuma Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Uonuma Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Uonuma Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Uonuma Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:subst:MAINSPACE]][[sn:Uonuma Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Uonuma Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Uonuma Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Uonuma Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[ja:魚沼神社]]

The vision inspired his troops and contributed to their subsequent victories. === The Rescued Soldier === During [[World War II]], a soldier who had fallen into a dry well was rescued by a white-haired elderly man holding a lantern marked with "Kami-Yahiko Shrine." Upon being reunited with his unit, the elderly man disappeared. === The Giant Cedar === A massive cedar tree known as "Ōki-sama" stood on the shrine grounds until it was toppled by a storm. Its trunk reportedly stretched down the mountain, serving as a makeshift bridge. == References == * Igarashi Shūtarō, ''Legends of Ojiya,'' Kōbunsha, 1985. * Ojiya City Historical Committee, ''Chronicle of Ojiya,'' Ojiya City Cultural Property Association, 1984. * Ojiya City Board of Education, ''History of Ojiya,'' 2007. == External links == * [http://www.city.ojiya.niigata.jp/soshiki/shogai/uonumajinja-amidado.html Uonuma Shrine Amida Hall] - Ojiya City Official Website * [https://niigata-kankou.or.jp/spot/6089 Niigata Tourism Guide: revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Uonuma Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Uonuma Shrine.JPG|270px]]
Main Hall | location = [[Niigata Prefecture]][[Ojiya, Niigata|Ojiya]] Tsuchikawa 2-chōme 699-1 | coordinates = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} | Enshrined deities = [[Amenokaguyama-no-Mikoto]] | Shrine rank = [[Shikinaisha]] (disputed), Traditional Second Shrine of [[Echigo Province]], Former [[Prefectural Shrine]] | consecration_year = Traditionally during the reign of [[Emperor Sujin]] | Main building = [[Nagare-zukuri]] | Alternate names = Kami-Yahiko Daimyōjin | Pilgrimage sites = | festival = [[September 7]] | Rituals = | Map = {{location map|Japan Niigata|label=Uonuma Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Uonuma Shrine''' (魚沼神社, ''Uonuma Jinja'') is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Ojiya, Niigata|Ojiya]], [[Niigata Prefecture]]. The shrine grounds include the nationally designated Important Cultural Property, the Amida Hall. == Enshrined deities == * [[Ame-no-Kaguyama]] * [[Toyotama-hime-no-Mikoto]] * Ahi-no-kami (Ahiko) == History == The shrine's foundation is traditionally attributed to the reign of [[Emperor Sujin]]. The name "Uonuma Shrine" appears in documents from the [[Heian period]] and is recorded in the ''[[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]'' among the five shrines in [[Uonuma District]]. However, its identification as the same Uonuma Shrine listed in the ''Engishiki'' has been debated since the [[Edo period]]. During the [[Sengoku period (Japan)|Sengoku period]], due to its shared deity with [[Yahiko Shrine]] in [[Yahiko, Niigata|Yahiko]], it was referred to as "Kami-Yahiko Shrine." Historical documents from the era of [[Uesugi Kenshin]] mention it as "Kami-Yahiko Daimyōjin," and a plaque reading "Yahiko Daimyōjin" is displayed in the worship hall. The shrine served as a religious center for the region and was later called "Uonuma Shrine" during the [[Bakumatsu]] period. It was relocated to its current location in Tsuchikawa from the earlier site at Kaminoyama. Key historical milestones include: * [[Jōgan 3]] (861 CE): Uonuma Shrine received the rank of [[Junior Fifth Rank|Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade]]. * [[Genkō 3]] (1333 CE): Shrine priests supported [[Nitta Yoshisada]] during his uprising. * [[Eikyō 9]] (1437 CE): The shrine received a large gong donated by Saikata Yasaburō Mitsuyuki (now a Niigata Prefecture-designated cultural property). * [[Eiroku 5]] (1562 CE): [[Uesugi Kenshin]] donated shrine lands and offerings for 18 branch shrines. * [[Eiroku 12]] (1569 CE): A hand-copied version of the ''[[Daihannya-kyō]]'' was donated to the shrine. * [[Meiji 6]] (1873 CE): Designated as a [[Gōsha]] and later promoted to [[Prefectural Shrine]]. == Shrine grounds == The main shrine gate is a [[Ryōbu torii]]. Adjacent to the purification fountain, stone steps lead to a historical water purification site. The shrine grounds include the Amida Hall to the left, the Kagura Hall to the right, and a monument commemorating the Russo-Japanese War nearby. ; Amida Hall The Amida Hall, constructed in [[1563 CE]] during the [[Muromachi period]], is designated as a National Important Cultural Property. It is a modest hōgyō-zukuri (hipped roof) structure with thatched roofing. Inside are statues of [[Amida Nyorai]] and [[Dainichi Nyorai]], said to have been crafted from iron sand sourced from the [[Shinano River]]. The hall was restored in [[1954 CE]] to its original form. == Cultural Properties == === National Important Cultural Properties === * Amida Hall - Designated under the former Ancient Shrines and Temples Preservation Act in [[1906 CE]]. === Niigata Prefecture-Designated Cultural Properties === * Large Gong - Crafted in [[1437 CE]] with inscriptions by Saikata Yasaburō Mitsuyuki. * ''Daihannya-kyō'' (559 scrolls) - Donated in [[1569 CE]]. === Ojiya City-Designated Cultural Properties === * Mikoshi - A portable shrine featuring a phoenix on its roof, originally crafted in [[1495 CE]] and restored in [[1817 CE]]. * ''Uonuma Shrine Annual Event Record'' - Historical documents outlining shrine activities. == Legends == === Uesugi Kenshin and the Divine Army === Uesugi Kenshin is said to have visited the shrine to pray for victory before crossing the [[Shinano River]] en route to the [[Kantō region]]. During one such visit, he reportedly saw a vision of [[Ame-no-Kaguyama]] and other deities marching toward Kantō. The vision inspired his troops and contributed to their subsequent victories. === The Rescued Soldier === During [[World War II]], a soldier who had fallen into a dry well was rescued by a white-haired elderly man holding a lantern marked with "Kami-Yahiko Shrine." Upon being reunited with his unit, the elderly man disappeared. === The Giant Cedar === A massive cedar tree known as "Ōki-sama" stood on the shrine grounds until it was toppled by a storm. Its trunk reportedly stretched down the mountain, serving as a makeshift bridge. == References == * Igarashi Shūtarō, ''Legends of Ojiya,'' Kōbunsha, 1985. * Ojiya City Historical Committee, ''Chronicle of Ojiya,'' Ojiya City Cultural Property Association, 1984. * Ojiya City Board of Education, ''History of Ojiya,'' 2007. == External links == * [http://www.city.ojiya.niigata.jp/soshiki/shogai/uonumajinja-amidado.html Uonuma Shrine Amida Hall] - Ojiya City Official Website * [https://niigata-kankou.or.jp/spot/6089 Niigata Tourism Guide: Uonuma Shrine Amida Hall] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Niigata Prefecture]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha]] [[Category:Syncretic shrines]] [[Category:Muromachi period architecture]] revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Uonuma Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Uonuma Shrine.JPG|270px]]
Main Hall | location = [[Niigata Prefecture]][[Ojiya, Niigata|Ojiya]] Tsuchikawa 2-chōme 699-1 | coordinates = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} | Enshrined deities = [[Amenokaguyama-no-Mikoto]] | Shrine rank = [[Shikinaisha]] (disputed), Traditional Second Shrine of [[Echigo Province]], Former [[Prefectural Shrine]] | consecration_year = Traditionally during the reign of [[Emperor Sujin]] | Main building = [[Nagare-zukuri]] | Alternate names = Kami-Yahiko Daimyōjin | Pilgrimage sites = | festival = [[September 7]] | Rituals = | Map = {{location map|Japan Niigata|label=Uonuma Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Uonuma Shrine''' (魚沼神社, ''Uonuma Jinja'') is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Ojiya, Niigata|Ojiya]], [[Niigata Prefecture]]. The shrine grounds include the nationally designated Important Cultural Property, the Amida Hall. == Enshrined deities == * [[Ame-no-Kaguyama]] * [[Toyotama-hime-no-Mikoto]] * Ahi-no-kami (Ahiko) == History == The shrine's foundation is traditionally attributed to the reign of [[Emperor Sujin]]. The name "Uonuma Shrine" appears in documents from the [[Heian period]] and is recorded in the ''[[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]'' among the five shrines in [[Uonuma District]]. However, its identification as the same Uonuma Shrine listed in the ''Engishiki'' has been debated since the [[Edo period]]. During the [[Sengoku period (Japan)|Sengoku period]], due to its shared deity with [[Yahiko Shrine]] in [[Yahiko, Niigata|Yahiko]], it was referred to as "Kami-Yahiko Shrine." Historical documents from the era of [[Uesugi Kenshin]] mention it as "Kami-Yahiko Daimyōjin," and a plaque reading "Yahiko Daimyōjin" is displayed in the worship hall. The shrine served as a religious center for the region and was later called "Uonuma Shrine" during the [[Bakumatsu]] period. It was relocated to its current location in Tsuchikawa from the earlier site at Kaminoyama. Key historical milestones include: * [[Jōgan 3]] (861 CE): Uonuma Shrine received the rank of [[Junior Fifth Rank|Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade]]. * [[Genkō 3]] (1333 CE): Shrine priests supported [[Nitta Yoshisada]] during his uprising. * [[Eikyō 9]] (1437 CE): The shrine received a large gong donated by Saikata Yasaburō Mitsuyuki (now a Niigata Prefecture-designated cultural property). * [[Eiroku 5]] (1562 CE): [[Uesugi Kenshin]] donated shrine lands and offerings for 18 branch shrines. * [[Eiroku 12]] (1569 CE): A hand-copied version of the ''[[Daihannya-kyō]]'' was donated to the shrine. * [[Meiji 6]] (1873 CE): Designated as a [[Gōsha]] and later promoted to [[Prefectural Shrine]]. == Shrine grounds == The main shrine gate is a [[Ryōbu torii]]. Adjacent to the purification fountain, stone steps lead to a historical water purification site. The shrine grounds include the Amida Hall to the left, the Kagura Hall to the right, and a monument commemorating the Russo-Japanese War nearby. ; Amida Hall The Amida Hall, constructed in [[1563 CE]] during the [[Muromachi period]], is designated as a National Important Cultural Property. It is a modest hōgyō-zukuri (hipped roof) structure with thatched roofing. Inside are statues of [[Amida Nyorai]] and [[Dainichi Nyorai]], said to have been crafted from iron sand sourced from the [[Shinano River]]. The hall was restored in [[1954 CE]] to its original form. == Cultural Properties == === National Important Cultural Properties === * Amida Hall - Designated under the former Ancient Shrines and Temples Preservation Act in [[1906 CE]]. === Niigata Prefecture-Designated Cultural Properties === * Large Gong - Crafted in [[1437 CE]] with inscriptions by Saikata Yasaburō Mitsuyuki. * ''Daihannya-kyō'' (559 scrolls) - Donated in [[1569 CE]]. === Ojiya City-Designated Cultural Properties === * Mikoshi - A portable shrine featuring a phoenix on its roof, originally crafted in [[1495 CE]] and restored in [[1817 CE]]. * ''Uonuma Shrine Annual Event Record'' - Historical documents outlining shrine activities. == Legends == === Uesugi Kenshin and the Divine Army === Uesugi Kenshin is said to have visited the shrine to pray for victory before crossing the [[Shinano River]] en route to the [[Kantō region]]. During one such visit, he reportedly saw a vision of [[Ame-no-Kaguyama]] and other deities marching toward Kantō. The vision inspired his troops and contributed to their subsequent victories. === The Rescued Soldier === During [[World War II]], a soldier who had fallen into a dry well was rescued by a white-haired elderly man holding a lantern marked with "Kami-Yahiko Shrine." Upon being reunited with his unit, the elderly man disappeared. === The Giant Cedar === A massive cedar tree known as "Ōki-sama" stood on the shrine grounds until it was toppled by a storm. Its trunk reportedly stretched down the mountain, serving as a makeshift bridge. == References == * Igarashi Shūtarō, ''Legends of Ojiya,'' Kōbunsha, 1985. * Ojiya City Historical Committee, ''Chronicle of Ojiya,'' Ojiya City Cultural Property Association, 1984. * Ojiya City Board of Education, ''History of Ojiya,'' 2007. == External links == * [http://www.city.ojiya.niigata.jp/soshiki/shogai/uonumajinja-amidado.html Uonuma Shrine Amida Hall] - Ojiya City Official Website * [https://niigata-kankou.or.jp/spot/6089 Niigata Tourism Guide: Uonuma Shrine Amida Hall] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Niigata Prefecture]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha]] [[Category:Syncretic shrines]] [[Category:Muromachi period architecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Cultural Properties of Niigata Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Ojiya, Niigata]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Ojiya, {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Uonuma Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Uonuma Shrine.JPG|270px]]
Main Hall | location = [[Niigata Prefecture]][[Ojiya, Niigata|Ojiya]] Tsuchikawa 2-chōme 699-1 | coordinates = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} | Enshrined deities = [[Amenokaguyama-no-Mikoto]] | Shrine rank = [[Shikinaisha]] (disputed), Traditional Second Shrine of [[Echigo Province]], Former [[Prefectural Shrine]] | consecration_year = Traditionally during the reign of [[Emperor Sujin]] | Main building = [[Nagare-zukuri]] | Alternate names = Kami-Yahiko Daimyōjin | Pilgrimage sites = | festival = [[September 7]] | Rituals = | Map = {{location map|Japan Niigata|label=Uonuma Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Uonuma Shrine''' (魚沼神社, ''Uonuma Jinja'') is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Ojiya, Niigata|Ojiya]], [[Niigata Prefecture]]. The shrine grounds include the nationally designated Important Cultural Property, the Amida Hall. == Enshrined deities == * [[Ame-no-Kaguyama]] * [[Toyotama-hime-no-Mikoto]] * Ahi-no-kami (Ahiko) == History == The shrine's foundation is traditionally attributed to the reign of [[Emperor Sujin]]. The name "Uonuma Shrine" appears in documents from the [[Heian period]] and is recorded in the ''[[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]'' among the five shrines in [[Uonuma District]]. However, its identification as the same Uonuma Shrine listed in the ''Engishiki'' has been debated since the [[Edo period]]. During the [[Sengoku period (Japan)|Sengoku period]], due to its shared deity with [[Yahiko Shrine]] in [[Yahiko, Niigata|Yahiko]], it was referred to as "Kami-Yahiko Shrine." Historical documents from the era of [[Uesugi Kenshin]] mention it as "Kami-Yahiko Daimyōjin," and a plaque reading "Yahiko Daimyōjin" is displayed in the worship hall. The shrine served as a religious center for the region and was later called "Uonuma Shrine" during the [[Bakumatsu]] period. It was relocated to its current location in Tsuchikawa from the earlier site at Kaminoyama. Key historical milestones include: * [[Jōgan 3]] (861 CE): Uonuma Shrine received the rank of [[Junior Fifth Rank|Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade]]. * [[Genkō 3]] (1333 CE): Shrine priests supported [[Nitta Yoshisada]] during his uprising. * [[Eikyō 9]] (1437 CE): The shrine received a large gong donated by Saikata Yasaburō Mitsuyuki (now a Niigata Prefecture-designated cultural property). * [[Eiroku 5]] (1562 CE): [[Uesugi Kenshin]] donated shrine lands and offerings for 18 branch shrines. * [[Eiroku 12]] (1569 CE): A hand-copied version of the ''[[Daihannya-kyō]]'' was donated to the shrine. * [[Meiji 6]] (1873 CE): Designated as a [[Gōsha]] and later promoted to [[Prefectural Shrine]]. == Shrine grounds == The main shrine gate is a [[Ryōbu torii]]. Adjacent to the purification fountain, stone steps lead to a historical water purification site. The shrine grounds include the Amida Hall to the left, the Kagura Hall to the right, and a monument commemorating the Russo-Japanese War nearby. ; Amida Hall The Amida Hall, constructed in [[1563 CE]] during the [[Muromachi period]], is designated as a National Important Cultural Property. It is a modest hōgyō-zukuri (hipped roof) structure with thatched roofing. Inside are statues of [[Amida Nyorai]] and [[Dainichi Nyorai]], said to have been crafted from iron sand sourced from the [[Shinano River]]. The hall was restored in [[1954 CE]] to its original form. == Cultural Properties == === National Important Cultural Properties === * Amida Hall - Designated under the former Ancient Shrines and Temples Preservation Act in [[1906 CE]]. === Niigata Prefecture-Designated Cultural Properties === * Large Gong - Crafted in [[1437 CE]] with inscriptions by Saikata Yasaburō Mitsuyuki. * ''Daihannya-kyō'' (559 scrolls) - Donated in [[1569 CE]]. === Ojiya City-Designated Cultural Properties === * Mikoshi - A portable shrine featuring a phoenix on its roof, originally crafted in [[1495 CE]] and restored in [[1817 CE]]. * ''Uonuma Shrine Annual Event Record'' - Historical documents outlining shrine activities. == Legends == === Uesugi Kenshin and the Divine Army === Uesugi Kenshin is said to have visited the shrine to pray for victory before crossing the [[Shinano River]] en route to the [[Kantō region]]. During one such visit, he reportedly saw a vision of [[Ame-no-Kaguyama]] and other deities marching toward Kantō. The vision inspired his troops and contributed to their subsequent victories. === The Rescued Soldier === During [[World War II]], a soldier who had fallen into a dry well was rescued by a white-haired elderly man holding a lantern marked with "Kami-Yahiko Shrine." Upon being reunited with his unit, the elderly man disappeared. === The Giant Cedar === A massive cedar tree known as "Ōki-sama" stood on the shrine grounds until it was toppled by a storm. Its trunk reportedly stretched down the mountain, serving as a makeshift bridge. == References == * Igarashi Shūtarō, ''Legends of Ojiya,'' Kōbunsha, 1985. * Ojiya City Historical Committee, ''Chronicle of Ojiya,'' Ojiya City Cultural Property Association, 1984. * Ojiya City Board of Education, ''History of Ojiya,'' 2007. == External links == * [http://www.city.ojiya.niigata.jp/soshiki/shogai/uonumajinja-amidado.html Uonuma Shrine Amida Hall] - Ojiya City Official Website * [https://niigata-kankou.or.jp/spot/6089 Niigata Tourism Guide: Uonuma Shrine Amida Hall] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Niigata Prefecture]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha]] [[Category:Syncretic shrines]] [[Category:Muromachi period architecture]] {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Uonuma Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Uonuma Shrine.JPG|270px]]
Main Hall | location = [[Niigata Prefecture]][[Ojiya, Niigata|Ojiya]] Tsuchikawa 2-chōme 699-1 | coordinates = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} | Enshrined deities = [[Amenokaguyama-no-Mikoto]] | Shrine rank = [[Shikinaisha]] (disputed), Traditional Second Shrine of [[Echigo Province]], Former [[Prefectural Shrine]] | consecration_year = Traditionally during the reign of [[Emperor Sujin]] | Main building = [[Nagare-zukuri]] | Alternate names = Kami-Yahiko Daimyōjin | Pilgrimage sites = | festival = [[September 7]] | Rituals = | Map = {{location map|Japan Niigata|label=Uonuma Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Uonuma Shrine''' (魚沼神社, ''Uonuma Jinja'') is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Ojiya, Niigata|Ojiya]], [[Niigata Prefecture]]. The shrine grounds include the nationally designated Important Cultural Property, the Amida Hall. == Enshrined deities == * [[Ame-no-Kaguyama]] * [[Toyotama-hime-no-Mikoto]] * Ahi-no-kami (Ahiko) == History == The shrine's foundation is traditionally attributed to the reign of [[Emperor Sujin]]. The name "Uonuma Shrine" appears in documents from the [[Heian period]] and is recorded in the ''[[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]'' among the five shrines in [[Uonuma District]]. However, its identification as the same Uonuma Shrine listed in the ''Engishiki'' has been debated since the [[Edo period]]. During the [[Sengoku period (Japan)|Sengoku period]], due to its shared deity with [[Yahiko Shrine]] in [[Yahiko, Niigata|Yahiko]], it was referred to as "Kami-Yahiko Shrine." Historical documents from the era of [[Uesugi Kenshin]] mention it as "Kami-Yahiko Daimyōjin," and a plaque reading "Yahiko Daimyōjin" is displayed in the worship hall. The shrine served as a religious center for the region and was later called "Uonuma Shrine" during the [[Bakumatsu]] period. It was relocated to its current location in Tsuchikawa from the earlier site at Kaminoyama. Key historical milestones include: * [[Jōgan 3]] (861 CE): Uonuma Shrine received the rank of [[Junior Fifth Rank|Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade]]. * [[Genkō 3]] (1333 CE): Shrine priests supported [[Nitta Yoshisada]] during his uprising. * [[Eikyō 9]] (1437 CE): The shrine received a large gong donated by Saikata Yasaburō Mitsuyuki (now a Niigata Prefecture-designated cultural property). * [[Eiroku 5]] (1562 CE): [[Uesugi Kenshin]] donated shrine lands and offerings for 18 branch shrines. * [[Eiroku 12]] (1569 CE): A hand-copied version of the ''[[Daihannya-kyō]]'' was donated to the shrine. * [[Meiji 6]] (1873 CE): Designated as a [[Gōsha]] and later promoted to [[Prefectural Shrine]]. == Shrine grounds == The main shrine gate is a [[Ryōbu torii]]. Adjacent to the purification fountain, stone steps lead to a historical water purification site. The shrine grounds include the Amida Hall to the left, the Kagura Hall to the right, and a monument commemorating the Russo-Japanese War nearby. ; Amida Hall The Amida Hall, constructed in [[1563 CE]] during the [[Muromachi period]], is designated as a National Important Cultural Property. It is a modest hōgyō-zukuri (hipped roof) structure with thatched roofing. Inside are statues of [[Amida Nyorai]] and [[Dainichi Nyorai]], said to have been crafted from iron sand sourced from the [[Shinano River]]. The hall was restored in [[1954 CE]] to its original form. == Cultural Properties == === National Important Cultural Properties === * Amida Hall - Designated under the former Ancient Shrines and Temples Preservation Act in [[1906 CE]]. === Niigata Prefecture-Designated Cultural Properties === * Large Gong - Crafted in [[1437 CE]] with inscriptions by Saikata Yasaburō Mitsuyuki. * ''Daihannya-kyō'' (559 scrolls) - Donated in [[1569 CE]]. === Ojiya City-Designated Cultural Properties === * Mikoshi - A portable shrine featuring a phoenix on its roof, originally crafted in [[1495 CE]] and restored in [[1817 CE]]. * ''Uonuma Shrine Annual Event Record'' - Historical documents outlining shrine activities. == Legends == === Uesugi Kenshin and the Divine Army === Uesugi Kenshin is said to have visited the shrine to pray for victory before crossing the [[Shinano River]] en route to the [[Kantō region]]. During one such visit, he reportedly saw a vision of [[Ame-no-Kaguyama]] and other deities marching toward Kantō. The vision inspired his troops and contributed to their subsequent victories. === The Rescued Soldier === During [[World War II]], a soldier who had fallen into a dry well was rescued by a white-haired elderly man holding a lantern marked with "Kami-Yahiko Shrine." Upon being reunited with his unit, the elderly man disappeared. === The Giant Cedar === A massive cedar tree known as "Ōki-sama" stood on the shrine grounds until it was toppled by a storm. Its trunk reportedly stretched down the mountain, serving as a makeshift bridge. == References == * Igarashi Shūtarō, ''Legends of Ojiya,'' Kōbunsha, 1985. * Ojiya City Historical Committee, ''Chronicle of Ojiya,'' Ojiya City Cultural Property Association, 1984. * Ojiya City Board of Education, ''History of Ojiya,'' 2007. == External links == * [http://www.city.ojiya.niigata.jp/soshiki/shogai/uonumajinja-amidado.html Uonuma Shrine Amida Hall] - Ojiya City Official Website * [https://niigata-kankou.or.jp/spot/6089 Niigata Tourism Guide: Uonuma Shrine Amida Hall] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Niigata Prefecture]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha]] [[Category:Syncretic shrines]] [[Category:Muromachi period architecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Cultural Properties of Niigata Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Ojiya, Niigata]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Ojiya, Niigata]] [[Category:魚沼神社]] [[Category:Default sort converted to citation needed]] {{citation needed|Uonuma Shrine}} [[Category:Completion tier 3]][[Category:tier 3]] [[Category:deprecated qq open]]

The hall was restored in [[1954 CE]] to its original form. == Cultural Properties == === National Important Cultural Properties === * Amida Hall - Designated under the former Ancient Shrines and Temples Preservation Act in [[1906 CE]]. === Niigata Prefecture-Designated Cultural Properties === * Large Gong - Crafted in [[1437 CE]] with inscriptions by Saikata Yasaburō Mitsuyuki. * ''Daihannya-kyō'' (559 scrolls) - Donated in [[1569 CE]]. === Ojiya City-Designated Cultural Properties === * Mikoshi - A portable shrine featuring a phoenix on its roof, originally crafted in [[1495 CE]] and restored in [[1817 CE]]. * ''Uonuma Shrine Annual Event Record'' - Historical documents outlining shrine activities. == Legends == === Uesugi Kenshin and the Divine Army === Uesugi Kenshin is said to have visited the shrine to pray for victory before crossing the [[Shinano River]] en route to the [[Kantō region]]. During one such visit, he reportedly saw a vision of [[Ame-no-Kaguyama]] and other deities marching toward Kantō. The vision inspired his troops and contributed to their subsequent victories. === The Rescued Soldier === During [[World War II]], a soldier who had fallen into a dry well was rescued by a white-haired elderly man holding a lantern marked with "Kami-Yahiko Shrine." Upon being reunited with his unit, the elderly man disappeared. === The Giant Cedar === A massive cedar tree known as "Ōki-sama" stood on the shrine grounds until it was toppled by a storm. Its trunk reportedly stretched down the mountain, serving as a makeshift bridge. == References == * Igarashi Shūtarō, ''Legends of Ojiya,'' Kōbunsha, 1985. * Ojiya City Historical Committee, ''Chronicle of Ojiya,'' Ojiya City Cultural Property Association, 1984. * Ojiya City Board of Education, ''History of Ojiya,'' 2007. == External links == * {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Uonuma Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Uonuma Shrine.JPG|270px]]
Main Hall | location = [[Niigata Prefecture]][[Ojiya, Niigata|Ojiya]] Tsuchikawa 2-chōme 699-1 | coordinates = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} | Enshrined deities = [[Amenokaguyama-no-Mikoto]] | Shrine rank = [[Shikinaisha]] (disputed), Traditional Second Shrine of [[Echigo Province]], Former [[Prefectural Shrine]] | consecration_year = Traditionally during the reign of [[Emperor Sujin]] | Main building = [[Nagare-zukuri]] | Alternate names = Kami-Yahiko Daimyōjin | Pilgrimage sites = | festival = [[September 7]] | Rituals = | Map = {{location map|Japan Niigata|label=Uonuma Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Uonuma Shrine''' (魚沼神社, ''Uonuma Jinja'') is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Ojiya, Niigata|Ojiya]], [[Niigata Prefecture]]. The shrine grounds include the nationally designated Important Cultural Property, the Amida Hall. == Enshrined deities == * [[Ame-no-Kaguyama]] * [[Toyotama-hime-no-Mikoto]] * Ahi-no-kami (Ahiko) == History == The shrine's foundation is traditionally attributed to the reign of [[Emperor Sujin]]. The name "Uonuma Shrine" appears in documents from the [[Heian period]] and is recorded in the ''[[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]'' among the five shrines in [[Uonuma District]]. However, its identification as the same Uonuma Shrine listed in the ''Engishiki'' has been debated since the [[Edo period]]. During the [[Sengoku period (Japan)|Sengoku period]], due to its shared deity with [[Yahiko Shrine]] in [[Yahiko, Niigata|Yahiko]], it was referred to as "Kami-Yahiko Shrine." Historical documents from the era of [[Uesugi Kenshin]] mention it as "Kami-Yahiko Daimyōjin," and a plaque reading "Yahiko Daimyōjin" is displayed in the worship hall. The shrine served as a religious center for the region and was later called "Uonuma Shrine" during the [[Bakumatsu]] period. It was relocated to its current location in Tsuchikawa from the earlier site at Kaminoyama. Key historical milestones include: * [[Jōgan 3]] (861 CE): Uonuma Shrine received the rank of [[Junior Fifth Rank|Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade]]. * [[Genkō 3]] (1333 CE): Shrine priests supported [[Nitta Yoshisada]] during his uprising. * [[Eikyō 9]] (1437 CE): The shrine received a large gong donated by Saikata Yasaburō Mitsuyuki (now a Niigata Prefecture-designated cultural property). * [[Eiroku 5]] (1562 CE): [[Uesugi Kenshin]] donated shrine lands and offerings for 18 branch shrines. * [[Eiroku 12]] (1569 CE): A hand-copied version of the ''[[Daihannya-kyō]]'' was donated to the shrine. * [[Meiji 6]] (1873 CE): Designated as a [[Gōsha]] and later promoted to [[Prefectural Shrine]]. == Shrine grounds == The main shrine gate is a [[Ryōbu torii]]. Adjacent to the purification fountain, stone steps lead to a historical water purification site. The shrine grounds include the Amida Hall to the left, the Kagura Hall to the right, and a monument commemorating the Russo-Japanese War nearby. ; Amida Hall The Amida Hall, constructed in [[1563 CE]] during the [[Muromachi period]], is designated as a National Important Cultural Property. It is a modest hōgyō-zukuri (hipped roof) structure with thatched roofing. Inside are statues of [[Amida Nyorai]] and [[Dainichi Nyorai]], said to have been crafted from iron sand sourced from the [[Shinano River]]. The hall was restored in [[1954 CE]] to its original form. == Cultural Properties == === National Important Cultural Properties === * Amida Hall - Designated under the former Ancient Shrines and Temples Preservation Act in [[1906 CE]]. === Niigata Prefecture-Designated Cultural Properties === * Large Gong - Crafted in [[1437 CE]] with inscriptions by Saikata Yasaburō Mitsuyuki. * ''Daihannya-kyō'' (559 scrolls) - Donated in [[1569 CE]]. === Ojiya City-Designated Cultural Properties === * Mikoshi - A portable shrine featuring a phoenix on its roof, originally crafted in [[1495 CE]] and restored in [[1817 CE]]. * ''Uonuma Shrine Annual Event Record'' - Historical documents outlining shrine activities. == Legends == === Uesugi Kenshin and the Divine Army === Uesugi Kenshin is said to have visited the shrine to pray for victory before crossing the [[Shinano River]] en route to the [[Kantō region]]. During one such visit, he reportedly saw a vision of [[Ame-no-Kaguyama]] and other deities marching toward Kantō. The vision inspired his troops and contributed to their subsequent victories. === The Rescued Soldier === During [[World War II]], a soldier who had fallen into a dry well was rescued by a white-haired elderly man holding a lantern marked with "Kami-Yahiko Shrine." Upon being reunited with his unit, the elderly man disappeared. === The Giant Cedar === A massive cedar tree known as "Ōki-sama" stood on the shrine grounds until it was toppled by a storm. Its trunk reportedly stretched down the mountain, serving as a makeshift bridge. == References == * Igarashi Shūtarō, ''Legends of Ojiya,'' Kōbunsha, 1985. * Ojiya City Historical Committee, ''Chronicle of Ojiya,'' Ojiya City Cultural Property Association, 1984. * Ojiya City Board of Education, ''History of Ojiya,'' 2007. == External links == * [http://www.city.ojiya.niigata.jp/soshiki/shogai/uonumajinja-amidado.html Uonuma Shrine Amida Hall] - Ojiya City Official Website * [https://niigata-kankou.or.jp/spot/6089 Niigata Tourism Guide: Uonuma Shrine Amida Hall] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Niigata Prefecture]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha]] [[Category:Syncretic shrines]] [[Category:Muromachi period architecture]] Ojiya City Official Website * [https://niigata-kankou.or.jp/spot/6089 Niigata Tourism Guide: Uonuma Shrine Amida Hall] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Niigata Prefecture]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha]] [[Category:Syncretic shrines]] [[Category:Muromachi period architecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Cultural Properties of Niigata Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Ojiya, Niigata]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Ojiya, Niigata]] [[Category:魚沼神社]] [[Category:Default sort converted to citation needed]] {{citation needed|Uonuma Shrine}} [[Category:Completion tier 3]][[Category:tier 3]] [[category:qq close]] [[Category:Uonuma Shrine]][[Category:Default sort converted to citation needed]] {{citation needed|Uonuma Shrine}} [[category:qq close]] [[Category:Uonuma Shrine]]{{citation needed|Uonuma Shrine}}[[Category:refreshing the redirects]] [[Category:mass bump]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[Category:nnn]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:qq]][[en:Uonuma Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[sn:Uonuma Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Uonuma Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Uonuma Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Uonuma Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Uonuma Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[sn:Uonuma Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Uonuma Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Uonuma Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Uonuma Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[sn:Uonuma Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Uonuma Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Uonuma Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Uonuma Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Uonuma Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[sn:Uonuma Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Uonuma Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Uonuma Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Uonuma Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[ja:魚沼神社]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Uonuma Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Uonuma Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]][[sn:onuma Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[sn:Uonuma Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:onuma Shrine]][[Category:qq]]

revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Uonuma Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Uonuma Shrine.JPG|270px]]
Main Hall | location = [[Niigata Prefecture]][[Ojiya, Niigata|Ojiya]] Tsuchikawa 2-chōme 699-1 | coordinates = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} | Enshrined deities = [[Amenokaguyama-no-Mikoto]] | Shrine rank = [[Shikinaisha]] (disputed), Traditional Second Shrine of [[Echigo Province]], Former [[Prefectural Shrine]] | consecration_year = Traditionally during the reign of [[Emperor Sujin]] | Main building = [[Nagare-zukuri]] | Alternate names = Kami-Yahiko Daimyōjin | Pilgrimage sites = | festival = [[September 7]] | Rituals = | Map = {{location map|Japan Niigata|label=Uonuma Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Uonuma Shrine''' (魚沼神社, ''Uonuma Jinja'') is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Ojiya, Niigata|Ojiya]], [[Niigata Prefecture]]. The shrine grounds include the nationally designated Important Cultural Property, the Amida Hall. == Enshrined deities == * [[Ame-no-Kaguyama]] * [[Toyotama-hime-no-Mikoto]] * Ahi-no-kami (Ahiko) == History == The shrine's foundation is traditionally attributed to the reign of [[Emperor Sujin]]. The name "Uonuma Shrine" appears in documents from the [[Heian period]] and is recorded in the ''[[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]'' among the five shrines in [[Uonuma District]]. However, its identification as the same Uonuma Shrine listed in the ''Engishiki'' has been debated since the [[Edo period]]. During the [[Sengoku period (Japan)|Sengoku period]], due to its shared deity with [[Yahiko Shrine]] in [[Yahiko, Niigata|Yahiko]], it was referred to as "Kami-Yahiko Shrine." Historical documents from the era of [[Uesugi Kenshin]] mention it as "Kami-Yahiko Daimyōjin," and a plaque reading "Yahiko Daimyōjin" is displayed in the worship hall. The shrine served as a religious center for the region and was later called "Uonuma Shrine" during the [[Bakumatsu]] period. It was relocated to its current location in Tsuchikawa from the earlier site at Kaminoyama. Key historical milestones include: * [[Jōgan 3]] (861 CE): Uonuma Shrine received the rank of [[Junior Fifth Rank|Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade]]. * [[Genkō 3]] (1333 CE): Shrine priests supported [[Nitta Yoshisada]] during his uprising. * [[Eikyō 9]] (1437 CE): The shrine received a large gong donated by Saikata Yasaburō Mitsuyuki (now a Niigata Prefecture-designated cultural property). * [[Eiroku 5]] (1562 CE): [[Uesugi Kenshin]] donated shrine lands and offerings for 18 branch shrines. * [[Eiroku 12]] (1569 CE): A hand-copied version of the ''[[Daihannya-kyō]]'' was donated to the shrine. * [[Meiji 6]] (1873 CE): Designated as a [[Gōsha]] and later promoted to [[Prefectural Shrine]]. == Shrine grounds == The main shrine gate is a [[Ryōbu torii]]. Adjacent to the purification fountain, stone steps lead to a historical water purification site. The shrine grounds include the Amida Hall to the left, the Kagura Hall to the right, and a monument commemorating the Russo-Japanese War nearby. ; Amida Hall The Amida Hall, constructed in [[1563 CE]] during the [[Muromachi period]], is designated as a National Important Cultural Property. It is a modest hōgyō-zukuri (hipped roof) structure with thatched roofing. Inside are statues of [[Amida Nyorai]] and [[Dainichi Nyorai]], said to have been crafted from iron sand sourced from the [[Shinano River]]. The hall was restored in [[1954 CE]] to its original form. == Cultural Properties == === National Important Cultural Properties === * Amida Hall - Designated under the former Ancient Shrines and Temples Preservation Act in [[1906 CE]]. === Niigata Prefecture-Designated Cultural Properties === * Large Gong - Crafted in [[1437 CE]] with inscriptions by Saikata Yasaburō Mitsuyuki. * ''Daihannya-kyō'' (559 scrolls) - Donated in [[1569 CE]]. === Ojiya City-Designated Cultural Properties === * Mikoshi - A portable shrine featuring a phoenix on its roof, originally crafted in [[1495 CE]] and restored in [[1817 CE]]. * ''Uonuma Shrine Annual Event Record'' - Historical documents outlining shrine activities. == Legends == === Uesugi Kenshin and the Divine Army === Uesugi Kenshin is said to have visited the shrine to pray for victory before crossing the [[Shinano River]] en route to the [[Kantō region]]. During one such visit, he reportedly saw a vision of [[Ame-no-Kaguyama]] and other deities marching toward Kantō. The vision inspired his troops and contributed to their subsequent victories. === The Rescued Soldier === During [[World War II]], a soldier who had fallen into a dry well was rescued by a white-haired elderly man holding a lantern marked with "Kami-Yahiko Shrine." Upon being reunited with his unit, the elderly man disappeared. === The Giant Cedar === A massive cedar tree known as "Ōki-sama" stood on the shrine grounds until it was toppled by a storm. Its trunk reportedly stretched down the mountain, serving as a makeshift bridge. == References == * Igarashi Shūtarō, ''Legends of Ojiya,'' Kōbunsha, 1985. * Ojiya City Historical Committee, ''Chronicle of Ojiya,'' Ojiya City Cultural Property Association, 1984. * Ojiya City Board of Education, ''History of Ojiya,'' 2007. == External links == * [http://www.city.ojiya.niigata.jp/soshiki/shogai/uonumajinja-amidado.html Uonuma Shrine Amida Hall] - Ojiya City Official Website * [https://niigata-kankou.or.jp/spot/6089 Niigata Tourism Guide: Uonuma Shrine Amida Hall] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Niigata Prefecture]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha]] [[Category:Syncretic shrines]] [[Category:Muromachi period architecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Cultural Properties of Niigata Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Ojiya, Niigata]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Ojiya, Niigata]] [[Category:魚沼神社]] [[Category:Default sort converted to citation needed]] {{citation needed|Uonuma Shrine}} [[Category:Completion tier 3]][[Category:tier 3]] [[category:qq close]] [[Category:Uonuma Shrine]][[Category:Default sort converted to citation needed]] {{citation needed|Uonuma Shrine}} [[category:qq close]] [[Category:Uonuma Shrine]]{{citation needed|Uonuma Shrine}}[[Category:refreshing the redirects]] [[Category:mass bump]] [[Category:nnn]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:qq]][[en:Uonuma Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[sn:Uonuma Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Uonuma Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Uonuma Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Uonuma Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Uonuma Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[sn:Uonuma Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Uonuma Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Uonuma Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Uonuma Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[sn:Uonuma Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Uonuma Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Uonuma Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Uonuma Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Uonuma Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[sn:Uonuma Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Uonuma Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Uonuma Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Uonuma Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[ja:魚沼神社]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Uonuma Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Uonuma Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]][[sn:onuma Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[sn:Uonuma Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:onuma Shrine]][[Category:qq]]
