Key historical milestones include: * [[Jōgan 3]] (861 CE): Uonuma Shrine received the Rank of [[Junior Fifth Rank|Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade]]. * [[Genkō 3]] (1333 CE): Shrine priests supported [[Nitta Yoshisada]] during his uprising. * [[Eikyō 9]] (1437 CE): The shrine received a large gong donated by Saikata Yasaburō Mitsuyuki (now a Niigata Prefecture-designated cultural property). * [[Eiroku 5]] (1562 CE): [[Uesugi Kenshin]] donated shrine lands and offerings for 18 branch shrines. * [[Eiroku 12]] (1569 CE): A hand-copied version of the ''[[Daihannya-kyō]]'' was donated to the shrine. * [[Meiji 6]] (1873 CE): Designated as a [[Gōsha]] and later promoted to [[Prefectural Shrine]]. == Shrine grounds == The main shrine gate is a [[Ryōbu torii]]. Adjacent to the purification fountain, stone steps lead to a historical water purification site. The shrine grounds include the Amida Hall to the left, the Kagura Hall to the right, and a monument commemorating the Russo-Japanese War nearby. ; Amida Hall The Amida Hall, constructed in [[1563 CE]] during the [[Muromachi period]], is designated as a National Important Cultural Property. It is a modest hōgyō-zukuri (hipped roof) structure with thatched roofing. Inside are statues of [[Amida Nyorai]] and [[Dainichi Nyorai]], said to have been crafted from iron sand sourced from the [[Shinano River]]. The hall was restored in [[1954 CE]] to its original form. == Cultural Properties == === National Important Cultural Properties === * Amida Hall - Designated under the former Ancient Shrines and Temples Preservation Act in [[1906 CE]]. === Niigata Prefecture-Designated Cultural Properties === * Large Gong - Crafted in [[1437 CE]] with inscriptions by Saikata Yasaburō Mitsuyuki. * ''Daihannya-kyō'' (559 scrolls) - Donated in [[1569 CE]]. === Ojiya City-Designated Cultural Properties === * Mikoshi - A portable shrine featuring a phoenix on its roof, originally crafted in [[1495 CE]] and restored in [[1817 CE]]. * ''Uonuma Shrine Annual Event Record'' - Historical documents outlining shrine activities. == Legends == === Uesugi Kenshin and the Divine Army === Uesugi Kenshin is said to have visited the shrine to pray for victory before crossing the [[Shinano River]] en route to the [[Kantō region]]. During one such visit, he reportedly saw a vision of [[Ame-no-Kaguyama]] and other deities marching toward Kantō. The vision inspired his troops and contributed to their subsequent victories. === The Rescued Soldier === During [[World War II]], a soldier who had fallen into a dry well was rescued by a white-haired elderly man holding a lantern marked with "Kami-Yahiko Shrine." Upon being reunited with his unit, the elderly man disappeared. === The Giant Cedar === A massive cedar tree known as "Ōki-sama" stood on the shrine grounds until it was toppled by a storm. Its trunk reportedly stretched down the mountain, serving as a makeshift bridge. == References == * Igarashi Shūtarō, ''Legends of Ojiya,'' Kōbunsha, 1985. * Ojiya City Historical Committee, ''Chronicle of Ojiya,'' Ojiya City Cultural Property Association, 1984. * Ojiya City Board of Education, ''History of Ojiya,'' 2007. == External links == * [http://www.city.ojiya.niigata.jp/soshiki/shogai/uonumajinja-amidado.html Uonuma Shrine Amida Hall] - Ojiya City Official Website * [https://niigata-kankou.or.jp/spot/6089 Niigata Tourism Guide: Uonuma Shrine Amida Hall] {{translated page|ja|魚沼神社|version=102256861|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Niigata Prefecture]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha]] [[Category:Syncretic shrines]] [[Category:Muromachi period architecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Cultural Properties of Niigata Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Ojiya, Niigata]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Ojiya, Niigata]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] }} [[en:Uonuma Shrine]] [[sn:Uonuma Shrine]] [[ja:魚沼神社]] [[sn:onuma Shrine]] [[Category:室町時代の建築]] [[Category:小千谷市の建築物]] [[Category:小千谷市の歴史]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Niigata Prefecture]] [[Category:新潟県の重要文化財]]

Upon being reunited with his unit, the elderly man disappeared. === The Giant Cedar === A massive cedar tree known as "Ōki-sama" stood on the shrine grounds until it was toppled by a storm. Key historical milestones include: * [[Jōgan 3]] (861 CE): Uonuma Shrine received the Rank of [[Junior Fifth Rank|Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade]]. * [[Genkō 3]] (1333 CE): Shrine priests supported [[Nitta Yoshisada]] during his uprising. * [[Eikyō 9]] (1437 CE): The shrine received a large gong donated by Saikata Yasaburō Mitsuyuki (now a Niigata Prefecture-designated cultural property). * [[Eiroku 5]] (1562 CE): [[Uesugi Kenshin]] donated shrine lands and offerings for 18 branch shrines. * [[Eiroku 12]] (1569 CE): A hand-copied version of the ''[[Daihannya-kyō]]'' was donated to the shrine. * [[Meiji 6]] (1873 CE): Designated as a [[Gōsha]] and later promoted to [[Prefectural Shrine]]. == Shrine grounds == The main shrine gate is a [[Ryōbu torii]]. Adjacent to the purification fountain, stone steps lead to a historical water purification site. The shrine grounds include the Amida Hall to the left, the Kagura Hall to the right, and a monument commemorating the Russo-Japanese War nearby. ; Amida Hall The Amida Hall, constructed in [[1563 CE]] during the [[Muromachi period]], is designated as a National Important Cultural Property. It is a modest hōgyō-zukuri (hipped roof) structure with thatched roofing. Inside are statues of [[Amida Nyorai]] and [[Dainichi Nyorai]], said to have been crafted from iron sand sourced from the [[Shinano River]]. The hall was restored in [[1954 CE]] to its original form. == Cultural Properties == === National Important Cultural Properties === * Amida Hall - Designated under the former Ancient Shrines and Temples Preservation Act in [[1906 CE]]. === Niigata Prefecture-Designated Cultural Properties === * Large Gong - Crafted in [[1437 CE]] with inscriptions by Saikata Yasaburō Mitsuyuki. * ''Daihannya-kyō'' (559 scrolls) - Donated in [[1569 CE]]. === Ojiya City-Designated Cultural Properties === * Mikoshi - A portable shrine featuring a phoenix on its roof, originally crafted in [[1495 CE]] and restored in [[1817 CE]]. * ''Uonuma Shrine Annual Event Record'' - Historical documents outlining shrine activities. == Legends == === Uesugi Kenshin and the Divine Army === Uesugi Kenshin is said to have visited the shrine to pray for victory before crossing the [[Shinano River]] en route to the [[Kantō region]]. During one such visit, he reportedly saw a vision of [[Ame-no-Kaguyama]] and other deities marching toward Kantō. The vision inspired his troops and contributed to their subsequent victories. === The Rescued Soldier === During [[World War II]], a soldier who had fallen into a dry well was rescued by a white-haired elderly man holding a lantern marked with "Kami-Yahiko Shrine." Upon being reunited with his unit, the elderly man disappeared. === The Giant Cedar === A massive cedar tree known as "Ōki-sama" stood on the shrine grounds until it was toppled by a storm. Its trunk reportedly stretched down the mountain, serving as a makeshift bridge. == References == * Igarashi Shūtarō, ''Legends of Ojiya,'' Kōbunsha, 1985. * Ojiya City Historical Committee, ''Chronicle of Ojiya,'' Ojiya City Cultural Property Association, 1984. * Ojiya City Board of Education, ''History of Ojiya,'' 2007. == External links == * [http://www.city.ojiya.niigata.jp/soshiki/shogai/uonumajinja-amidado.html Uonuma Shrine Amida Hall] - Ojiya City Official Website * [https://niigata-kankou.or.jp/spot/6089 Niigata Tourism Guide: Uonuma Shrine Amida Hall] {{translated page|ja|魚沼神社|version=102256861|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Niigata Prefecture]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha]] [[Category:Syncretic shrines]] [[Category:Muromachi period architecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Cultural Properties of Niigata Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Ojiya, Niigata]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Ojiya, Niigata]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] }} [[en:Uonuma Shrine]] [[sn:Uonuma Shrine]] [[ja:魚沼神社]] [[sn:onuma Shrine]] [[Category:室町時代の建築]] [[Category:小千谷市の建築物]] [[Category:小千谷市の歴史]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Niigata Prefecture]] [[Category:新潟県の重要文化財]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Echigo Province]] [[Category:Cultural heritage monuments in Japan]]

Key historical milestones include: * [[Jōgan 3]] (861 CE): Uonuma Shrine received the Rank of [[Junior Fifth Rank|Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade]]. * [[Genkō 3]] (1333 CE): Shrine priests supported [[Nitta Yoshisada]] during his uprising. * [[Eikyō 9]] (1437 CE): The shrine received a large gong donated by Saikata Yasaburō Mitsuyuki (now a Niigata Prefecture-designated cultural property). * [[Eiroku 5]] (1562 CE): [[Uesugi Kenshin]] donated shrine lands and offerings for 18 branch shrines. * [[Eiroku 12]] (1569 CE): A hand-copied version of the ''[[Daihannya-kyō]]'' was donated to the shrine. * [[Meiji 6]] (1873 CE): Designated as a [[Gōsha]] and later promoted to [[Prefectural Shrine]]. == Shrine grounds == The main shrine gate is a [[Ryōbu torii]]. Adjacent to the purification fountain, stone steps lead to a historical water purification site. The shrine grounds include the Amida Hall to the left, the Kagura Hall to the right, and a monument commemorating the Russo-Japanese War nearby. ; Amida Hall The Amida Hall, constructed in [[1563 CE]] during the [[Muromachi period]], is designated as a National Important Cultural Property. It is a modest hōgyō-zukuri (hipped roof) structure with thatched roofing. Inside are statues of [[Amida Nyorai]] and [[Dainichi Nyorai]], said to have been crafted from iron sand sourced from the [[Shinano River]]. The hall was restored in [[1954 CE]] to its original form. == Cultural Properties == === National Important Cultural Properties === * Amida Hall - Designated under the former Ancient Shrines and Temples Preservation Act in [[1906 CE]]. === Niigata Prefecture-Designated Cultural Properties === * Large Gong - Crafted in [[1437 CE]] with inscriptions by Saikata Yasaburō Mitsuyuki. * ''Daihannya-kyō'' (559 scrolls) - Donated in [[1569 CE]]. === Ojiya City-Designated Cultural Properties === * Mikoshi - A portable shrine featuring a phoenix on its roof, originally crafted in [[1495 CE]] and restored in [[1817 CE]]. * ''Uonuma Shrine Annual Event Record'' - Historical documents outlining shrine activities. == Legends == === Uesugi Kenshin and the Divine Army === Uesugi Kenshin is said to have visited the shrine to pray for victory before crossing the [[Shinano River]] en route to the [[Kantō region]]. During one such visit, he reportedly saw a vision of [[Ame-no-Kaguyama]] and other deities marching toward Kantō. The vision inspired his troops and contributed to their subsequent victories. === The Rescued Soldier === During [[World War II]], a soldier who had fallen into a dry well was rescued by a white-haired elderly man holding a lantern marked with "Kami-Yahiko Shrine." Upon being reunited with his unit, the elderly man disappeared. === The Giant Cedar === A massive cedar tree known as "Ōki-sama" stood on the shrine grounds until it was toppled by a storm. Its trunk reportedly stretched down the mountain, serving as a makeshift bridge. == References == * Igarashi Shūtarō, ''Legends of Ojiya,'' Kōbunsha, 1985. * Ojiya City Historical Committee, ''Chronicle of Ojiya,'' Ojiya City Cultural Property Association, 1984. * Ojiya City Board of Education, ''History of Ojiya,'' 2007. == External links == * [http://www.city.ojiya.niigata.jp/soshiki/shogai/uonumajinja-amidado.html Uonuma Shrine Amida Hall] - Ojiya City Official Website * [https://niigata-kankou.or.jp/spot/6089 Niigata Tourism Guide: Uonuma Shrine Amida Hall] {{translated page|ja|魚沼神社|version=102256861|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Niigata Prefecture]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha]] [[Category:Syncretic shrines]] [[Category:Muromachi period architecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Cultural Properties of Niigata Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Ojiya, Niigata]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Ojiya, Niigata]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] }} [[en:Uonuma Shrine]] [[sn:Uonuma Shrine]] [[ja:魚沼神社]] [[sn:onuma Shrine]] [[Category:Architecture of Muromachi period]] [[Category:Buildings in Ojiya, Niigata]] [[Category:History of Ojiya]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Niigata Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Property of Japan in Niigata prefecture]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Echigo Province]] [[Category:Cultural heritage monuments in Japan]]

Key historical milestones include: * [[Jōgan 3]] (861 CE): Uonuma Shrine received the Rank of [[Junior Fifth Rank|Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade]]. * [[Genkō 3]] (1333 CE): Shrine priests supported [[Nitta Yoshisada]] during his uprising. * [[Eikyō 9]] (1437 CE): The shrine received a large gong donated by Saikata Yasaburō Mitsuyuki (now a Niigata Prefecture-designated cultural property). * [[Eiroku 5]] (1562 CE): [[Uesugi Kenshin]] donated shrine lands and offerings for 18 branch shrines. * [[Eiroku 12]] (1569 CE): A hand-copied version of the ''[[Daihannya-kyō]]'' was donated to the shrine. * [[Meiji 6]] (1873 CE): Designated as a [[Gōsha]] and later promoted to [[Prefectural Shrine]]. == Shrine grounds == The main shrine gate is a [[Ryōbu torii]]. Adjacent to the purification fountain, stone steps lead to a historical water purification site. The shrine grounds include the Amida Hall to the left, the Kagura Hall to the right, and a monument commemorating the Russo-Japanese War nearby. ; Amida Hall The Amida Hall, constructed in [[1563 CE]] during the [[Muromachi period]], is designated as a National Important Cultural Property. It is a modest hōgyō-zukuri (hipped roof) structure with thatched roofing. Inside are statues of [[Amida Nyorai]] and [[Dainichi Nyorai]], said to have been crafted from iron sand sourced from the [[Shinano River]]. The hall was restored in [[1954 CE]] to its original form. == Cultural Properties == === National Important Cultural Properties === * Amida Hall - Designated under the former Ancient Shrines and Temples Preservation Act in [[1906 CE]]. === Niigata Prefecture-Designated Cultural Properties === * Large Gong - Crafted in [[:en:1437 CE|1437 CE]] with inscriptions by Saikata Yasaburō Mitsuyuki. * ''Daihannya-kyō'' (559 scrolls) - Donated in [[1569 CE]]. === Ojiya City-Designated Cultural Properties === * Mikoshi - A portable shrine featuring a phoenix on its roof, originally crafted in [[:en:1495 CE|1495 CE]] and restored in [[1817 CE]]. * ''Uonuma Shrine Annual Event Record'' - Historical documents outlining shrine activities. == Legends == === Uesugi Kenshin and the Divine Army === Uesugi Kenshin is said to have visited the shrine to pray for victory before crossing the [[Shinano River]] en route to the [[Kantō region]]. During one such visit, he reportedly saw a vision of [[Ame-no-Kaguyama]] and other deities marching toward Kantō. The vision inspired his troops and contributed to their subsequent victories. === The Rescued Soldier === During [[World War II]], a soldier who had fallen into a dry well was rescued by a white-haired elderly man holding a lantern marked with "Kami-Yahiko Shrine." Upon being reunited with his unit, the elderly man disappeared. === The Giant Cedar === A massive cedar tree known as "Ōki-sama" stood on the shrine grounds until it was toppled by a storm. Its trunk reportedly stretched down the mountain, serving as a makeshift bridge. == References == * Igarashi Shūtarō, ''Legends of Ojiya,'' Kōbunsha, 1985. * Ojiya City Historical Committee, ''Chronicle of Ojiya,'' Ojiya City Cultural Property Association, 1984. * Ojiya City Board of Education, ''History of Ojiya,'' 2007. == External links == * [http://www.city.ojiya.niigata.jp/soshiki/shogai/uonumajinja-amidado.html Uonuma Shrine Amida Hall] - Ojiya City Official Website * [https://niigata-kankou.or.jp/spot/6089 Niigata Tourism Guide: Uonuma Shrine Amida Hall] {{translated page|ja|魚沼神社|version=102256861|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Niigata Prefecture]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha]] [[Category:Syncretic shrines]] [[Category:Muromachi period architecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Cultural Properties of Niigata Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Ojiya, Niigata]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Ojiya, Niigata]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] }} [[sn:Uonuma Shrine]] [[ja:魚沼神社]] [[sn:onuma Shrine]] [[Category:Architecture of Muromachi period]] [[Category:Buildings in Ojiya, Niigata]] [[Category:History of Ojiya]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Niigata Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Property of Japan in Niigata prefecture]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Echigo Province]] [[Category:Cultural heritage monuments in Japan]]

Key historical milestones include: * [[Jōgan 3]] (861 CE): Uonuma Shrine received the Rank of [[Junior Fifth Rank|Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade]]. * [[Genkō 3]] (1333 CE): Shrine priests supported [[Nitta Yoshisada]] during his uprising. * [[Eikyō 9]] (1437 CE): The shrine received a large gong donated by Saikata Yasaburō Mitsuyuki (now a Niigata Prefecture-designated cultural property). * [[Eiroku 5]] (1562 CE): [[Uesugi Kenshin]] donated shrine lands and offerings for 18 branch shrines. * [[Eiroku 12]] (1569 CE): A hand-copied version of the ''[[Daihannya-kyō]]'' was donated to the shrine. * [[Meiji 6]] (1873 CE): Designated as a [[Gōsha]] and later promoted to [[Prefectural Shrine]]. == Shrine grounds == The main shrine gate is a [[Ryōbu torii]]. Adjacent to the purification fountain, stone steps lead to a historical water purification site. The shrine grounds include the Amida Hall to the left, the Kagura Hall to the right, and a monument commemorating the Russo-Japanese War nearby. ; Amida Hall The Amida Hall, constructed in [[:en:1563 CE|1563 CE]] during the [[Muromachi period]], is designated as a National Important Cultural Property. It is a modest hōgyō-zukuri (hipped roof) structure with thatched roofing. Inside are statues of [[Amida Nyorai]] and [[Dainichi Nyorai]], said to have been crafted from iron sand sourced from the [[Shinano River]]. The hall was restored in [[1954 CE]] to its original form. == Cultural Properties == === National Important Cultural Properties === * Amida Hall - Designated under the former Ancient Shrines and Temples Preservation Act in [[1906 CE]]. === Niigata Prefecture-Designated Cultural Properties === * Large Gong - Crafted in [[:en:1437 CE|1437 CE]] with inscriptions by Saikata Yasaburō Mitsuyuki. * ''Daihannya-kyō'' (559 scrolls) - Donated in [[:en:1569 CE|1569 CE]]. === Ojiya City-Designated Cultural Properties === * Mikoshi - A portable shrine featuring a phoenix on its roof, originally crafted in [[:en:1495 CE|1495 CE]] and restored in [[:en:1817 CE|1817 CE]]. * ''Uonuma Shrine Annual Event Record'' - Historical documents outlining shrine activities. == Legends == === Uesugi Kenshin and the Divine Army === Uesugi Kenshin is said to have visited the shrine to pray for victory before crossing the [[Shinano River]] en route to the [[Kantō region]]. During one such visit, he reportedly saw a vision of [[Ame-no-Kaguyama]] and other deities marching toward Kantō. The vision inspired his troops and contributed to their subsequent victories. === The Rescued Soldier === During [[World War II]], a soldier who had fallen into a dry well was rescued by a white-haired elderly man holding a lantern marked with "Kami-Yahiko Shrine." Upon being reunited with his unit, the elderly man disappeared. === The Giant Cedar === A massive cedar tree known as "Ōki-sama" stood on the shrine grounds until it was toppled by a storm. Its trunk reportedly stretched down the mountain, serving as a makeshift bridge. == References == * Igarashi Shūtarō, ''Legends of Ojiya,'' Kōbunsha, 1985. * Ojiya City Historical Committee, ''Chronicle of Ojiya,'' Ojiya City Cultural Property Association, 1984. * Ojiya City Board of Education, ''History of Ojiya,'' 2007. == External links == * [http://www.city.ojiya.niigata.jp/soshiki/shogai/uonumajinja-amidado.html Uonuma Shrine Amida Hall] - Ojiya City Official Website * [https://niigata-kankou.or.jp/spot/6089 Niigata Tourism Guide: Uonuma Shrine Amida Hall] {{translated page|ja|魚沼神社|version=102256861|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Niigata Prefecture]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha]] [[Category:Syncretic shrines]] [[Category:Muromachi period architecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Cultural Properties of Niigata Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Ojiya, Niigata]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] }} [[sn:Uonuma Shrine]] [[ja:魚沼神社]] [[sn:onuma Shrine]] [[Category:Architecture of Muromachi period]] [[Category:Buildings in Ojiya, Niigata]] [[Category:History of Ojiya]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Niigata Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Property of Japan in Niigata prefecture]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Echigo Province]] [[Category:Cultural heritage monuments in Japan]]

Inside are statues of [[Amida Nyorai]] and [[Dainichi Nyorai]], said to have been crafted from iron sand sourced from the [[Shinano River]]. During one such visit, he reportedly saw a vision of [[Ame-no-Kaguyama]] and other deities marching toward Kantō. The vision inspired his troops and contributed to their subsequent victories. === The Rescued Soldier === During [[World War II]], a soldier who had fallen into a dry well was rescued by a white-haired elderly man holding a lantern marked with "Kami-Yahiko Shrine." Upon being reunited with his unit, the elderly man disappeared. === The Giant Cedar === A massive cedar tree known as "Ōki-sama" stood on the shrine grounds until it was toppled by a storm. Its trunk reportedly stretched down the mountain, serving as a makeshift bridge. == References == * Igarashi Shūtarō, ''Legends of Ojiya,'' Kōbunsha, 1985. * Ojiya City Historical Committee, ''Chronicle of Ojiya,'' Ojiya City Cultural Property Association, 1984. * Ojiya City Board of Education, ''History of Ojiya,'' 2007. == External links == * [http://www.city.ojiya.niigata.jp/soshiki/shogai/uonumajinja-amidado.html Uonuma Shrine Amida Hall] - Ojiya City Official Website * [https://niigata-kankou.or.jp/spot/6089 Niigata Tourism Guide: Uonuma Shrine Amida Hall] {{translated page|ja|魚沼神社|version=102256861|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Niigata Prefecture]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha]] [[Category:Syncretic shrines]] [[Category:Muromachi period architecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Cultural Properties of Niigata Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Ojiya, Niigata]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] }} [[sn:Uonuma Shrine]] [[ja:魚沼神社]] [[sn:onuma Shrine]] [[Category:Architecture of Muromachi period]] [[Category:Buildings in Ojiya, Niigata]] [[Category:History of Ojiya]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Niigata Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Property of Japan in Niigata prefecture]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Echigo Province]] [[Category:Cultural heritage monuments in Japan]]

Key historical milestones include: * [[Jōgan 3]] (861 CE): Uonuma Shrine received the Rank of [[Junior Fifth Rank|Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade]]. * [[Genkō 3]] (1333 CE): Shrine priests supported [[Nitta Yoshisada]] during his uprising. * [[Eikyō 9]] (1437 CE): The shrine received a large gong donated by Saikata Yasaburō Mitsuyuki (now a Niigata Prefecture-designated cultural property). * [[Eiroku 5]] (1562 CE): [[Uesugi Kenshin]] donated shrine lands and offerings for 18 branch shrines. * [[Eiroku 12]] (1569 CE): A hand-copied version of the ''[[Daihannya-kyō]]'' was donated to the shrine. * [[Meiji 6]] (1873 CE): Designated as a [[Gōsha]] and later promoted to [[Prefectural Shrine]]. == Shrine grounds == The main shrine gate is a [[Ryōbu torii]]. Adjacent to the purification fountain, stone steps lead to a historical water purification site. The shrine grounds include the Amida Hall to the left, the Kagura Hall to the right, and a monument commemorating the Russo-Japanese War nearby. ; Amida Hall The Amida Hall, constructed in [[:en:1563 CE|1563 CE]] during the [[Muromachi period]], is designated as a National Important Cultural Property. It is a modest hōgyō-zukuri (hipped roof) structure with thatched roofing. Inside are statues of [[Amida Nyorai]] and [[Dainichi Nyorai]], said to have been crafted from iron sand sourced from the [[Shinano River]]. The hall was restored in [[:en:1954 CE|1954 CE]] to its original form. == Cultural Properties == === National Important Cultural Properties === * Amida Hall - Designated under the former Ancient Shrines and Temples Preservation Act in [[:en:1906 CE|1906 CE]]. === Niigata Prefecture-Designated Cultural Properties === * Large Gong - Crafted in [[:en:1437 CE|1437 CE]] with inscriptions by Saikata Yasaburō Mitsuyuki. * ''Daihannya-kyō'' (559 scrolls) - Donated in [[:en:1569 CE|1569 CE]]. === Ojiya City-Designated Cultural Properties === * Mikoshi - A portable shrine featuring a phoenix on its roof, originally crafted in [[:en:1495 CE|1495 CE]] and restored in [[:en:1817 CE|1817 CE]]. * ''Uonuma Shrine Annual Event Record'' - Historical documents outlining shrine activities. == Legends == === Uesugi Kenshin and the Divine Army === Uesugi Kenshin is said to have visited the shrine to pray for victory before crossing the [[Shinano River]] en route to the [[Kantō region]]. During one such visit, he reportedly saw a vision of [[Ame-no-Kaguyama]] and other deities marching toward Kantō. The vision inspired his troops and contributed to their subsequent victories. === The Rescued Soldier === During [[World War II]], a soldier who had fallen into a dry well was rescued by a white-haired elderly man holding a lantern marked with "Kami-Yahiko Shrine." Upon being reunited with his unit, the elderly man disappeared. === The Giant Cedar === A massive cedar tree known as "Ōki-sama" stood on the shrine grounds until it was toppled by a storm. Its trunk reportedly stretched down the mountain, serving as a makeshift bridge. == References == * Igarashi Shūtarō, ''Legends of Ojiya,'' Kōbunsha, 1985. * Ojiya City Historical Committee, ''Chronicle of Ojiya,'' Ojiya City Cultural Property Association, 1984. * Ojiya City Board of Education, ''History of Ojiya,'' 2007. == External links == * [http://www.city.ojiya.niigata.jp/soshiki/shogai/uonumajinja-amidado.html Uonuma Shrine Amida Hall] - Ojiya City Official Website * [https://niigata-kankou.or.jp/spot/6089 Niigata Tourism Guide: Uonuma Shrine Amida Hall] {{translated page|ja|魚沼神社|version=102256861|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Niigata Prefecture]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha]] [[Category:Syncretic shrines]] [[Category:Muromachi period architecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] }} [[sn:Uonuma Shrine]] [[ja:魚沼神社]] [[sn:onuma Shrine]] [[Category:Architecture of Muromachi period]] [[Category:Buildings in Ojiya, Niigata]] [[Category:History of Ojiya]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Niigata Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Property of Japan in Niigata prefecture]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Echigo Province]] [[Category:Cultural heritage monuments in Japan]]

Upon being reunited with his unit, the elderly man disappeared. === The Giant Cedar === A massive cedar tree known as "Ōki-sama" stood on the shrine grounds until it was toppled by a storm. Key historical milestones include: * [[Jōgan 3]] (861 CE): Uonuma Shrine received the Rank of [[Junior Fifth Rank|Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade]]. * [[Genkō 3]] (1333 CE): Shrine priests supported [[Nitta Yoshisada]] during his uprising. * [[Eikyō 9]] (1437 CE): The shrine received a large gong donated by Saikata Yasaburō Mitsuyuki (now a Niigata Prefecture-designated cultural property). * [[Eiroku 5]] (1562 CE): [[Uesugi Kenshin]] donated shrine lands and offerings for 18 branch shrines. * [[Eiroku 12]] (1569 CE): A hand-copied version of the ''[[Daihannya-kyō]]'' was donated to the shrine. * [[Meiji 6]] (1873 CE): Designated as a [[Gōsha]] and later promoted to [[Prefectural Shrine]]. == Shrine grounds == The main shrine gate is a [[Ryōbu torii]]. Adjacent to the purification fountain, stone steps lead to a historical water purification site. The shrine grounds include the Amida Hall to the left, the Kagura Hall to the right, and a monument commemorating the Russo-Japanese War nearby. ; Amida Hall The Amida Hall, constructed in [[:en:1563 CE|1563 CE]] during the [[Muromachi period]], is designated as a National Important Cultural Property. It is a modest hōgyō-zukuri (hipped roof) structure with thatched roofing. Inside are statues of [[:en:Amida Nyorai|Amida Nyorai]] and [[:en:Dainichi Nyorai|Dainichi Nyorai]], said to have been crafted from iron sand sourced from the [[Shinano River]]. The hall was restored in [[:en:1954 CE|1954 CE]] to its original form. == Cultural Properties == === National Important Cultural Properties === * Amida Hall - Designated under the former Ancient Shrines and Temples Preservation Act in [[:en:1906 CE|1906 CE]]. === Niigata Prefecture-Designated Cultural Properties === * Large Gong - Crafted in [[:en:1437 CE|1437 CE]] with inscriptions by Saikata Yasaburō Mitsuyuki. * ''Daihannya-kyō'' (559 scrolls) - Donated in [[:en:1569 CE|1569 CE]]. === Ojiya City-Designated Cultural Properties === * Mikoshi - A portable shrine featuring a phoenix on its roof, originally crafted in [[:en:1495 CE|1495 CE]] and restored in [[:en:1817 CE|1817 CE]]. * ''Uonuma Shrine Annual Event Record'' - Historical documents outlining shrine activities. == Legends == === Uesugi Kenshin and the Divine Army === Uesugi Kenshin is said to have visited the shrine to pray for victory before crossing the [[Shinano River]] en route to the [[Kantō region]]. During one such visit, he reportedly saw a vision of [[Ame-no-Kaguyama]] and other deities marching toward Kantō. The vision inspired his troops and contributed to their subsequent victories. === The Rescued Soldier === During [[World War II]], a soldier who had fallen into a dry well was rescued by a white-haired elderly man holding a lantern marked with "Kami-Yahiko Shrine." Upon being reunited with his unit, the elderly man disappeared. === The Giant Cedar === A massive cedar tree known as "Ōki-sama" stood on the shrine grounds until it was toppled by a storm. Its trunk reportedly stretched down the mountain, serving as a makeshift bridge. == References == * Igarashi Shūtarō, ''Legends of Ojiya,'' Kōbunsha, 1985. * Ojiya City Historical Committee, ''Chronicle of Ojiya,'' Ojiya City Cultural Property Association, 1984. * Ojiya City Board of Education, ''History of Ojiya,'' 2007. == External links == * [http://www.city.ojiya.niigata.jp/soshiki/shogai/uonumajinja-amidado.html Uonuma Shrine Amida Hall] - Ojiya City Official Website * [https://niigata-kankou.or.jp/spot/6089 Niigata Tourism Guide: Uonuma Shrine Amida Hall] {{translated page|ja|魚沼神社|version=102256861|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Niigata Prefecture]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha]] [[Category:Syncretic shrines]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] }} [[sn:Uonuma Shrine]] [[ja:魚沼神社]] [[sn:onuma Shrine]] [[Category:Architecture of Muromachi period]] [[Category:Buildings in Ojiya, Niigata]] [[Category:History of Ojiya]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Niigata Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Property of Japan in Niigata prefecture]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Echigo Province]] [[Category:Cultural heritage monuments in Japan]]

During one such visit, he reportedly saw a vision of [[Ame-no-Kaguyama]] and other deities marching toward Kantō. The vision inspired his troops and contributed to their subsequent victories. === The Rescued Soldier === During [[World War II]], a soldier who had fallen into a dry well was rescued by a white-haired elderly man holding a lantern marked with "Kami-Yahiko Shrine." Upon being reunited with his unit, the elderly man disappeared. === The Giant Cedar === A massive cedar tree known as "Ōki-sama" stood on the shrine grounds until it was toppled by a storm. Its trunk reportedly stretched down the mountain, serving as a makeshift bridge. == References == * Igarashi Shūtarō, ''Legends of Ojiya,'' Kōbunsha, 1985. * Ojiya City Historical Committee, ''Chronicle of Ojiya,'' Ojiya City Cultural Property Association, 1984. * Ojiya City Board of Education, ''History of Ojiya,'' 2007. == External links == * [http://www.city.ojiya.niigata.jp/soshiki/shogai/uonumajinja-amidado.html Uonuma Shrine Amida Hall] - Ojiya City Official Website * [https://niigata-kankou.or.jp/spot/6089 Niigata Tourism Guide: Uonuma Shrine Amida Hall] {{translated page|ja|魚沼神社|version=102256861|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Niigata Prefecture]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha]] [[Category:Syncretic shrines]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] }} [[sn:Uonuma Shrine]] [[ja:魚沼神社]] [[sn:onuma Shrine]] [[Category:Architecture of Muromachi period]] [[Category:Buildings in Ojiya, Niigata]] [[Category:History of Ojiya]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Niigata Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Property of Japan in Niigata prefecture]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Echigo Province]] [[Category:Cultural heritage monuments in Japan]]

Key historical milestones include: * [[Jōgan 3]] (861 CE): Uonuma Shrine received the Rank of [[Junior Fifth Rank|Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade]]. * [[Genkō 3]] (1333 CE): Shrine priests supported [[Nitta Yoshisada]] during his uprising. * [[Eikyō 9]] (1437 CE): The shrine received a large gong donated by Saikata Yasaburō Mitsuyuki (now a Niigata Prefecture-designated cultural property). * [[Eiroku 5]] (1562 CE): [[Uesugi Kenshin]] donated shrine lands and offerings for 18 branch shrines. * [[Eiroku 12]] (1569 CE): A hand-copied version of the ''[[Daihannya-kyō]]'' was donated to the shrine. * [[Meiji 6]] (1873 CE): Designated as a [[Gōsha]] and later promoted to [[Prefectural Shrine]]. == Shrine grounds == The main shrine gate is a [[Ryōbu torii]]. Adjacent to the purification fountain, stone steps lead to a historical water purification site. The shrine grounds include the Amida Hall to the left, the Kagura Hall to the right, and a monument commemorating the Russo-Japanese War nearby. ; Amida Hall The Amida Hall, constructed in [[:en:1563 CE|1563 CE]] during the [[Muromachi period]], is designated as a National Important Cultural Property. It is a modest hōgyō-zukuri (hipped roof) structure with thatched roofing. Inside are statues of [[:en:Amida Nyorai|Amida Nyorai]] and [[:en:Dainichi Nyorai|Dainichi Nyorai]], said to have been crafted from iron sand sourced from the [[Shinano River]]. The hall was restored in [[:en:1954 CE|1954 CE]] to its original form. == Cultural Properties == === National Important Cultural Properties === * Amida Hall - Designated under the former Ancient Shrines and Temples Preservation Act in [[:en:1906 CE|1906 CE]]. === Niigata Prefecture-Designated Cultural Properties === * Large Gong - Crafted in [[:en:1437 CE|1437 CE]] with inscriptions by Saikata Yasaburō Mitsuyuki. * ''Daihannya-kyō'' (559 scrolls) - Donated in [[:en:1569 CE|1569 CE]]. === Ojiya City-Designated Cultural Properties === * Mikoshi - A portable shrine featuring a phoenix on its roof, originally crafted in [[:en:1495 CE|1495 CE]] and restored in [[:en:1817 CE|1817 CE]]. * ''Uonuma Shrine Annual Event Record'' - Historical documents outlining shrine activities. == Legends == === Uesugi Kenshin and the Divine Army === Uesugi Kenshin is said to have visited the shrine to pray for victory before crossing the [[Shinano River]] en route to the [[Kantō region]]. During one such visit, he reportedly saw a vision of [[Ame-no-Kaguyama]] and other deities marching toward Kantō. The vision inspired his troops and contributed to their subsequent victories. === The Rescued Soldier === During [[World War II]], a soldier who had fallen into a dry well was rescued by a white-haired elderly man holding a lantern marked with "Kami-Yahiko Shrine." Upon being reunited with his unit, the elderly man disappeared. === The Giant Cedar === A massive cedar tree known as "Ōki-sama" stood on the shrine grounds until it was toppled by a storm. Its trunk reportedly stretched down the mountain, serving as a makeshift bridge. == References == * Igarashi Shūtarō, ''Legends of Ojiya,'' Kōbunsha, 1985. * Ojiya City Historical Committee, ''Chronicle of Ojiya,'' Ojiya City Cultural Property Association, 1984. * Ojiya City Board of Education, ''History of Ojiya,'' 2007. == External links == * [http://www.city.ojiya.niigata.jp/soshiki/shogai/uonumajinja-amidado.html Uonuma Shrine Amida Hall] - Ojiya City Official Website * [https://niigata-kankou.or.jp/spot/6089 Niigata Tourism Guide: Uonuma Shrine Amida Hall] {{translated page|ja|魚沼神社|version=102256861|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Niigata Prefecture]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] }} [[sn:Uonuma Shrine]] [[ja:魚沼神社]] [[sn:onuma Shrine]] [[Category:Architecture of Muromachi period]] [[Category:Buildings in Ojiya, Niigata]] [[Category:History of Ojiya]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Niigata Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Property of Japan in Niigata prefecture]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Echigo Province]] [[Category:Cultural heritage monuments in Japan]]

During one such visit, he reportedly saw a vision of [[Ame-no-Kaguyama]] and other deities marching toward Kantō. The vision inspired his troops and contributed to their subsequent victories. === The Rescued Soldier === During [[World War II]], a soldier who had fallen into a dry well was rescued by a white-haired elderly man holding a lantern marked with "Kami-Yahiko Shrine." Upon being reunited with his unit, the elderly man disappeared. === The Giant Cedar === A massive cedar tree known as "Ōki-sama" stood on the shrine grounds until it was toppled by a storm. Its trunk reportedly stretched down the mountain, serving as a makeshift bridge. == References == * Igarashi Shūtarō, ''Legends of Ojiya,'' Kōbunsha, 1985. * Ojiya City Historical Committee, ''Chronicle of Ojiya,'' Ojiya City Cultural Property Association, 1984. * Ojiya City Board of Education, ''History of Ojiya,'' 2007. == External links == * [http://www.city.ojiya.niigata.jp/soshiki/shogai/uonumajinja-amidado.html Uonuma Shrine Amida Hall] - Ojiya City Official Website * [https://niigata-kankou.or.jp/spot/6089 Niigata Tourism Guide: Uonuma Shrine Amida Hall] {{translated page|ja|魚沼神社|version=102256861|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Niigata Prefecture]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] }} [[sn:Uonuma Shrine]] [[ja:魚沼神社]] [[sn:onuma Shrine]] [[Category:Architecture of Muromachi period]] [[Category:Buildings in Ojiya, Niigata]] [[Category:History of Ojiya]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Niigata Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Property of Japan in Niigata prefecture]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Echigo Province]] [[Category:Cultural heritage monuments in Japan]]

The shrine served as a religious center for the region and was later called "Uonuma Shrine" during the [[:en:Bakumatsu|Bakumatsu]] period. It was relocated to its current location in Tsuchikawa from the earlier site at Kaminoyama. Key historical milestones include: * [[Jōgan 3]] (861 CE): Uonuma Shrine received the Rank of [[Junior Fifth Rank|Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade]]. * [[Genkō 3]] (1333 CE): Shrine priests supported [[Nitta Yoshisada]] during his uprising. * [[Eikyō 9]] (1437 CE): The shrine received a large gong donated by Saikata Yasaburō Mitsuyuki (now a Niigata Prefecture-designated cultural property). * [[Eiroku 5]] (1562 CE): [[Uesugi Kenshin]] donated shrine lands and offerings for 18 branch shrines. * [[Eiroku 12]] (1569 CE): A hand-copied version of the ''[[Daihannya-kyō]]'' was donated to the shrine. * [[:en:Meiji 6|Meiji 6]] (1873 CE): Designated as a [[Gōsha]] and later promoted to [[Prefectural Shrine]]. == Shrine grounds == The main shrine gate is a [[Ryōbu torii]]. Adjacent to the purification fountain, stone steps lead to a historical water purification site. The shrine grounds include the Amida Hall to the left, the Kagura Hall to the right, and a monument commemorating the Russo-Japanese War nearby. ; Amida Hall The Amida Hall, constructed in [[:en:1563 CE|1563 CE]] during the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], is designated as a National Important Cultural Property. It is a modest hōgyō-zukuri (hipped roof) structure with thatched roofing. Inside are statues of [[:en:Amida Nyorai|Amida Nyorai]] and [[:en:Dainichi Nyorai|Dainichi Nyorai]], said to have been crafted from iron sand sourced from the [[Shinano River]]. The hall was restored in [[:en:1954 CE|1954 CE]] to its original form. == Cultural Properties == === National Important Cultural Properties === * Amida Hall - Designated under the former Ancient Shrines and Temples Preservation Act in [[:en:1906 CE|1906 CE]]. === Niigata Prefecture-Designated Cultural Properties === * Large Gong - Crafted in [[:en:1437 CE|1437 CE]] with inscriptions by Saikata Yasaburō Mitsuyuki. * ''Daihannya-kyō'' (559 scrolls) - Donated in [[:en:1569 CE|1569 CE]]. === Ojiya City-Designated Cultural Properties === * Mikoshi - A portable shrine featuring a phoenix on its roof, originally crafted in [[:en:1495 CE|1495 CE]] and restored in [[:en:1817 CE|1817 CE]]. * ''Uonuma Shrine Annual Event Record'' - Historical documents outlining shrine activities. == Legends == === Uesugi Kenshin and the Divine Army === Uesugi Kenshin is said to have visited the shrine to pray for victory before crossing the [[Shinano River]] en route to the [[:en:Kantō region|Kantō region]]. During one such visit, he reportedly saw a vision of [[Ame-no-Kaguyama]] and other deities marching toward Kantō. The vision inspired his troops and contributed to their subsequent victories. === The Rescued Soldier === During [[World War II]], a soldier who had fallen into a dry well was rescued by a white-haired elderly man holding a lantern marked with "Kami-Yahiko Shrine." Upon being reunited with his unit, the elderly man disappeared. === The Giant Cedar === A massive cedar tree known as "Ōki-sama" stood on the shrine grounds until it was toppled by a storm. Its trunk reportedly stretched down the mountain, serving as a makeshift bridge. == References == * Igarashi Shūtarō, ''Legends of Ojiya,'' Kōbunsha, 1985. * Ojiya City Historical Committee, ''Chronicle of Ojiya,'' Ojiya City Cultural Property Association, 1984. * Ojiya City Board of Education, ''History of Ojiya,'' 2007. == External links == * [http://www.city.ojiya.niigata.jp/soshiki/shogai/uonumajinja-amidado.html Uonuma Shrine Amida Hall] - Ojiya City Official Website * [https://niigata-kankou.or.jp/spot/6089 Niigata Tourism Guide: Uonuma Shrine Amida Hall] {{translated page|ja|魚沼神社|version=102256861|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Niigata Prefecture]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] }} [[sn:Uonuma Shrine]] [[ja:魚沼神社]] [[sn:onuma Shrine]] [[Category:Architecture of Muromachi period]] [[Category:Buildings in Ojiya, Niigata]] [[Category:History of Ojiya]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Niigata Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Property of Japan in Niigata prefecture]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Echigo Province]] [[Category:Cultural heritage monuments in Japan]]

Main Hall | location = [[:en:Niigata Prefecture|Niigata Prefecture]][[:en:Ojiya, Niigata|Ojiya]] Tsuchikawa 2-chōme 699-1 | coordinates = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} | Enshrined deities = [[Amenokaguyama-no-Mikoto]] | Shrine Rank = [[Shikinaisha]] (disputed), Traditional Second Shrine of [[Echigo Province]], Former [[Prefectural Shrine]] | consecration_year = Traditionally during the reign of [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]] | Main building = [[Nagare-zukuri]] | alternate_name = Kami-Yahiko Daimyōjin | Pilgrimage sites = | festival = [[September 7]] | Rituals = | Map = {{location map|Japan Niigata|label=Uonuma Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Uonuma Shrine''' (魚沼神社, ''Uonuma Jinja'') is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Ojiya, Niigata|Ojiya]], [[:en:Niigata Prefecture|Niigata Prefecture]]. The shrine grounds include the nationally designated Important Cultural Property, the Amida Hall. == Enshrined deities == * [[Ame-no-Kaguyama]] * [[Toyotama-hime-no-Mikoto]] * Ahi-no-kami (Ahiko) == History == The shrine's foundation is traditionally attributed to the reign of [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]]. The name "Uonuma Shrine" appears in documents from the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] and is recorded in the ''[[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]'' among the five shrines in [[Uonuma District]]. However, its identification as the same Uonuma Shrine listed in the ''Engishiki'' has been debated since the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]]. During the [[Sengoku period (Japan)|Sengoku period]], due to its shared deity with [[Yahiko Shrine]] in [[:en:Yahiko, Niigata|Yahiko]], it was referred to as "Kami-Yahiko Shrine." Historical documents from the era of [[:en:Uesugi Kenshin|Uesugi Kenshin]] mention it as "Kami-Yahiko Daimyōjin," and a plaque reading "Yahiko Daimyōjin" is displayed in the worship hall. The shrine served as a religious center for the region and was later called "Uonuma Shrine" during the [[:en:Bakumatsu|Bakumatsu]] period. It was relocated to its current location in Tsuchikawa from the earlier site at Kaminoyama. Key historical milestones include: * [[Jōgan 3]] (861 CE): Uonuma Shrine received the Rank of [[Junior Fifth Rank|Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade]]. * [[Genkō 3]] (1333 CE): Shrine priests supported [[Nitta Yoshisada]] during his uprising. * [[Eikyō 9]] (1437 CE): The shrine received a large gong donated by Saikata Yasaburō Mitsuyuki (now a Niigata Prefecture-designated cultural property). * [[Eiroku 5]] (1562 CE): [[:en:Uesugi Kenshin|Uesugi Kenshin]] donated shrine lands and offerings for 18 branch shrines. * [[Eiroku 12]] (1569 CE): A hand-copied version of the ''[[Daihannya-kyō]]'' was donated to the shrine. * [[:en:Meiji 6|Meiji 6]] (1873 CE): Designated as a [[Gōsha]] and later promoted to [[Prefectural Shrine]]. == Shrine grounds == The main shrine gate is a [[Ryōbu torii]]. Adjacent to the purification fountain, stone steps lead to a historical water purification site. The shrine grounds include the Amida Hall to the left, the Kagura Hall to the right, and a monument commemorating the Russo-Japanese War nearby. ; Amida Hall The Amida Hall, constructed in [[:en:1563 CE|1563 CE]] during the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], is designated as a National Important Cultural Property. It is a modest hōgyō-zukuri (hipped roof) structure with thatched roofing. Inside are statues of [[:en:Amida Nyorai|Amida Nyorai]] and [[:en:Dainichi Nyorai|Dainichi Nyorai]], said to have been crafted from iron sand sourced from the [[Shinano River]]. The hall was restored in [[:en:1954 CE|1954 CE]] to its original form. == Cultural Properties == === National Important Cultural Properties === * Amida Hall - Designated under the former Ancient Shrines and Temples Preservation Act in [[:en:1906 CE|1906 CE]]. === Niigata Prefecture-Designated Cultural Properties === * Large Gong - Crafted in [[:en:1437 CE|1437 CE]] with inscriptions by Saikata Yasaburō Mitsuyuki. * ''Daihannya-kyō'' (559 scrolls) - Donated in [[:en:1569 CE|1569 CE]]. === Ojiya City-Designated Cultural Properties === * Mikoshi - A portable shrine featuring a phoenix on its roof, originally crafted in [[:en:1495 CE|1495 CE]] and restored in [[:en:1817 CE|1817 CE]]. * ''Uonuma Shrine Annual Event Record'' - Historical documents outlining shrine activities. == Legends == === Uesugi Kenshin and the Divine Army === Uesugi Kenshin is said to have visited the shrine to pray for victory before crossing the [[Shinano River]] en route to the [[:en:Kantō region|Kantō region]]. During one such visit, he reportedly saw a vision of [[Ame-no-Kaguyama]] and other deities marching toward Kantō. The vision inspired his troops and contributed to their subsequent victories. === The Rescued Soldier === During [[:en:Pacific War|Pacific War]], a soldier who had fallen into a dry well was rescued by a white-haired elderly man holding a lantern marked with "Kami-Yahiko Shrine." Upon being reunited with his unit, the elderly man disappeared. === The Giant Cedar === A massive cedar tree known as "Ōki-sama" stood on the shrine grounds until it was toppled by a storm. Its trunk reportedly stretched down the mountain, serving as a makeshift bridge. == References == * Igarashi Shūtarō, ''Legends of Ojiya,'' Kōbunsha, 1985. * Ojiya City Historical Committee, ''Chronicle of Ojiya,'' Ojiya City Cultural Property Association, 1984. * Ojiya City Board of Education, ''History of Ojiya,'' 2007. == External links == * [http://www.city.ojiya.niigata.jp/soshiki/shogai/uonumajinja-amidado.html Uonuma Shrine Amida Hall] - Ojiya City Official Website * [https://niigata-kankou.or.jp/spot/6089 Niigata Tourism Guide: Uonuma Shrine Amida Hall] {{translated page|ja|魚沼神社|version=102256861|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Niigata Prefecture]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] }} [[sn:Uonuma Shrine]] [[ja:魚沼神社]] [[sn:onuma Shrine]] [[Category:Architecture of Muromachi period]] [[Category:Buildings in Ojiya, Niigata]] [[Category:History of Ojiya]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Niigata Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Property of Japan in Niigata prefecture]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Echigo Province]] [[Category:Cultural heritage monuments in Japan]]

The hall was restored in [[:en:1954 CE|1954 CE]] to its original form. == Cultural Properties == === National Important Cultural Properties === * Amida Hall - Designated under the former Ancient Shrines and Temples Preservation Act in [[:en:1906 CE|1906 CE]]. === Niigata Prefecture-Designated Cultural Properties === * Large Gong - Crafted in [[:en:1437 CE|1437 CE]] with inscriptions by Saikata Yasaburō Mitsuyuki. * ''Daihannya-kyō'' (559 scrolls) - Donated in [[:en:1569 CE|1569 CE]]. === Ojiya City-Designated Cultural Properties === * Mikoshi - A portable shrine featuring a phoenix on its roof, originally crafted in [[:en:1495 CE|1495 CE]] and restored in [[:en:1817 CE|1817 CE]]. * ''Uonuma Shrine Annual Event Record'' - Historical documents outlining shrine activities. == Legends == === Uesugi Kenshin and the Divine Army === Uesugi Kenshin is said to have visited the shrine to pray for victory before crossing the [[:en:Shinano River|Shinano River]] en route to the [[:en:Kantō region|Kantō region]]. During one such visit, he reportedly saw a vision of [[Ame-no-Kaguyama]] and other deities marching toward Kantō. The vision inspired his troops and contributed to their subsequent victories. === The Rescued Soldier === During [[:en:Pacific War|Pacific War]], a soldier who had fallen into a dry well was rescued by a white-haired elderly man holding a lantern marked with "Kami-Yahiko Shrine." Upon being reunited with his unit, the elderly man disappeared. === The Giant Cedar === A massive cedar tree known as "Ōki-sama" stood on the shrine grounds until it was toppled by a storm. Its trunk reportedly stretched down the mountain, serving as a makeshift bridge. == References == * Igarashi Shūtarō, ''Legends of Ojiya,'' Kōbunsha, 1985. * Ojiya City Historical Committee, ''Chronicle of Ojiya,'' Ojiya City Cultural Property Association, 1984. * Ojiya City Board of Education, ''History of Ojiya,'' 2007. == External links == * [http://www.city.ojiya.niigata.jp/soshiki/shogai/uonumajinja-amidado.html Uonuma Shrine Amida Hall] - Ojiya City Official Website * [https://niigata-kankou.or.jp/spot/6089 Niigata Tourism Guide: Uonuma Shrine Amida Hall] {{translated page|ja|魚沼神社|version=102256861|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Niigata Prefecture]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] }} [[sn:Uonuma Shrine]] [[ja:魚沼神社]] [[sn:onuma Shrine]] [[Category:Architecture of Muromachi period]] [[Category:Buildings in Ojiya, Niigata]] [[Category:History of Ojiya]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Niigata Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Property of Japan in Niigata prefecture]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Echigo Province]] [[Category:Cultural heritage monuments in Japan]]

{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Uonuma Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Uonuma Shrine.JPG|270px]]
Main Hall | location = [[:en:Niigata Prefecture|Niigata Prefecture]][[:en:Ojiya, Niigata|Ojiya]] Tsuchikawa 2-chōme 699-1 | coordinates = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} | Enshrined deities = [[Amenokaguyama-no-Mikoto]] | Shrine Rank = {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|qq=draft|lt=Shikinaisha}} (disputed), Traditional Second Shrine of [[Echigo Province]], Former [[Prefectural Shrine]] | consecration_year = Traditionally during the reign of [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]] | Main building = [[Nagare-zukuri]] | alternate_name = Kami-Yahiko Daimyōjin | Pilgrimage sites = | festival = [[:en:September 7|September 7]] | Rituals = | Map = {{location map|Japan Niigata|label=Uonuma Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Uonuma Shrine''' (魚沼神社, ''Uonuma Jinja'') is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Ojiya, Niigata|Ojiya]], [[:en:Niigata Prefecture|Niigata Prefecture]]. The shrine grounds include the nationally designated Important Cultural Property, the Amida Hall. == Enshrined deities == * [[Ame-no-Kaguyama]] * [[Toyotama-hime-no-Mikoto]] * Ahi-no-kami (Ahiko) == History == The shrine's foundation is traditionally attributed to the reign of [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]]. The name "Uonuma Shrine" appears in documents from the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] and is recorded in the ''[[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]'' among the five shrines in [[Uonuma District]]. However, its identification as the same Uonuma Shrine listed in the ''Engishiki'' has been debated since the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]]. During the [[Sengoku period (Japan)|Sengoku period]], due to its shared deity with [[Yahiko Shrine]] in [[:en:Yahiko, Niigata|Yahiko]], it was referred to as "Kami-Yahiko Shrine." Historical documents from the era of [[:en:Uesugi Kenshin|Uesugi Kenshin]] mention it as "Kami-Yahiko Daimyōjin," and a plaque reading "Yahiko Daimyōjin" is displayed in the worship hall. The shrine served as a religious center for the region and was later called "Uonuma Shrine" during the [[:en:Bakumatsu|Bakumatsu]] period. It was relocated to its current location in Tsuchikawa from the earlier site at Kaminoyama. Key historical milestones include: * [[Jōgan 3]] (861 CE): Uonuma Shrine received the Rank of [[Junior Fifth Rank|Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade]]. * [[Genkō 3]] (1333 CE): Shrine priests supported [[Nitta Yoshisada]] during his uprising. * [[Eikyō 9]] (1437 CE): The shrine received a large gong donated by Saikata Yasaburō Mitsuyuki (now a Niigata Prefecture-designated cultural property). * [[Eiroku 5]] (1562 CE): [[:en:Uesugi Kenshin|Uesugi Kenshin]] donated shrine lands and offerings for 18 branch shrines. * [[Eiroku 12]] (1569 CE): A hand-copied version of the ''[[Daihannya-kyō]]'' was donated to the shrine. * [[:en:Meiji 6|Meiji 6]] (1873 CE): Designated as a [[Gōsha]] and later promoted to [[Prefectural Shrine]]. == Shrine grounds == The main shrine gate is a [[Ryōbu torii]]. Adjacent to the purification fountain, stone steps lead to a historical water purification site. The shrine grounds include the Amida Hall to the left, the Kagura Hall to the right, and a monument commemorating the Russo-Japanese War nearby. ; Amida Hall The Amida Hall, constructed in [[:en:1563 CE|1563 CE]] during the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], is designated as a National Important Cultural Property. It is a modest hōgyō-zukuri (hipped roof) structure with thatched roofing. Inside are statues of [[:en:Amida Nyorai|Amida Nyorai]] and [[:en:Dainichi Nyorai|Dainichi Nyorai]], said to have been crafted from iron sand sourced from the [[:en:Shinano River|Shinano River]]. The hall was restored in [[:en:1954 CE|1954 CE]] to its original form. == Cultural Properties == === National Important Cultural Properties === * Amida Hall - Designated under the former Ancient Shrines and Temples Preservation Act in [[:en:1906 CE|1906 CE]]. === Niigata Prefecture-Designated Cultural Properties === * Large Gong - Crafted in [[:en:1437 CE|1437 CE]] with inscriptions by Saikata Yasaburō Mitsuyuki. * ''Daihannya-kyō'' (559 scrolls) - Donated in [[:en:1569 CE|1569 CE]]. === Ojiya City-Designated Cultural Properties === * Mikoshi - A portable shrine featuring a phoenix on its roof, originally crafted in [[:en:1495 CE|1495 CE]] and restored in [[:en:1817 CE|1817 CE]]. * ''Uonuma Shrine Annual Event Record'' - Historical documents outlining shrine activities. == Legends == === Uesugi Kenshin and the Divine Army === Uesugi Kenshin is said to have visited the shrine to pray for victory before crossing the [[:en:Shinano River|Shinano River]] en route to the [[:en:Kantō region|Kantō region]]. During one such visit, he reportedly saw a vision of [[Ame-no-Kaguyama]] and other deities marching toward Kantō. The vision inspired his troops and contributed to their subsequent victories. === The Rescued Soldier === During [[:en:Pacific War|Pacific War]], a soldier who had fallen into a dry well was rescued by a white-haired elderly man holding a lantern marked with "Kami-Yahiko Shrine." Upon being reunited with his unit, the elderly man disappeared. === The Giant Cedar === A massive cedar tree known as "Ōki-sama" stood on the shrine grounds until it was toppled by a storm. Its trunk reportedly stretched down the mountain, serving as a makeshift bridge. == References == * Igarashi Shūtarō, ''Legends of Ojiya,'' Kōbunsha, 1985. * Ojiya City Historical Committee, ''Chronicle of Ojiya,'' Ojiya City Cultural Property Association, 1984. * Ojiya City Board of Education, ''History of Ojiya,'' 2007. == External links == * [http://www.city.ojiya.niigata.jp/soshiki/shogai/uonumajinja-amidado.html Uonuma Shrine Amida Hall] - Ojiya City Official Website * [https://niigata-kankou.or.jp/spot/6089 Niigata Tourism Guide: Uonuma Shrine Amida Hall] {{translated page|ja|魚沼神社|version=102256861|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Niigata Prefecture]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] }} [[sn:Uonuma Shrine]] [[ja:魚沼神社]] [[sn:onuma Shrine]] [[Category:Architecture of Muromachi period]] [[Category:Buildings in Ojiya, Niigata]] [[Category:History of Ojiya]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Niigata Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Property of Japan in Niigata prefecture]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Echigo Province]] [[Category:Cultural heritage monuments in Japan]]

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11674000]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:16, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Uonuma Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = File:Uonuma Shrine.JPG | location = [[:en:Niigata Prefecture|Niigata Prefecture]][[:en:Ojiya, Niigata|Ojiya]] Tsuchikawa 2-chōme 699-1 | coordinates = {{Coord|37.305|N|138.78772194444446|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | Enshrined deities = [[Amenokaguyama-no-Mikoto]] | Shrine Rank = {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|qq=draft|lt=Shikinaisha}} (disputed), Traditional Second Shrine of [[Echigo Province]], Former [[Prefectural Shrine]] | consecration_year = Traditionally during the reign of [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]] | Main building = [[Nagare-zukuri]] | alternate_name = Kami-Yahiko Daimyōjin | Pilgrimage sites = | festival = [[:en:September 7|September 7]] | Rituals = | Map = {{location map|Japan Niigata|label=Uonuma Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} | prefecture = {{ill|Ojiya|qid=Q819174}} }}
'''Uonuma Shrine''' (魚沼神社, ''Uonuma Jinja'') is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Ojiya, Niigata|Ojiya]], [[:en:Niigata Prefecture|Niigata Prefecture]]. The shrine grounds include the nationally designated Important Cultural Property, the Amida Hall.
== Enshrined deities == * [[Ame-no-Kaguyama]] * [[:en:Toyotama-hime|Toyotama-hime]] * Ahi-no-kami (Ahiko)
== History == The shrine's foundation is traditionally attributed to the reign of [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]]. The name "Uonuma Shrine" appears in documents from the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] and is recorded in the ''[[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]'' among the five shrines in [[Uonuma District]]. However, its identification as the same Uonuma Shrine listed in the ''Engishiki'' has been debated since the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]].
During the [[Sengoku period (Japan)|Sengoku period]], due to its shared deity with [[Yahiko Shrine]] in [[:en:Yahiko, Niigata|Yahiko]], it was referred to as "Kami-Yahiko Shrine." Historical documents from the era of [[:en:Uesugi Kenshin|Uesugi Kenshin]] mention it as "Kami-Yahiko Daimyōjin," and a plaque reading "Yahiko Daimyōjin" is displayed in the worship hall. The shrine served as a religious center for the region and was later called "Uonuma Shrine" during the [[:en:Bakumatsu|Bakumatsu]] period. It was relocated to its current location in Tsuchikawa from the earlier site at Kaminoyama.
Key historical milestones include: * [[Jōgan 3]] (861 CE): Uonuma Shrine received the Rank of [[Junior Fifth Rank|Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade]]. * [[Genkō 3]] (1333 CE): Shrine priests supported [[Nitta Yoshisada]] during his uprising. * [[Eikyō 9]] (1437 CE): The shrine received a large gong donated by Saikata Yasaburō Mitsuyuki (now a Niigata Prefecture-designated cultural property). * [[Eiroku 5]] (1562 CE): [[:en:Uesugi Kenshin|Uesugi Kenshin]] donated shrine lands and offerings for 18 branch shrines. * [[Eiroku 12]] (1569 CE): A hand-copied version of the ''[[Daihannya-kyō]]'' was donated to the shrine. * [[:en:Meiji 6|Meiji 6]] (1873 CE): Designated as a [[Gōsha]] and later promoted to [[Prefectural Shrine]].
== Shrine grounds == The main shrine gate is a [[Ryōbu torii]]. Adjacent to the purification fountain, stone steps lead to a historical water purification site. The shrine grounds include the Amida Hall to the left, the Kagura Hall to the right, and a monument commemorating the Russo-Japanese War nearby.
; Amida Hall The Amida Hall, constructed in [[:en:1563 CE|1563 CE]] during the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], is designated as a National Important Cultural Property. It is a modest hōgyō-zukuri (hipped roof) structure with thatched roofing. Inside are statues of [[:en:Amida Nyorai|Amida Nyorai]] and [[:en:Dainichi Nyorai|Dainichi Nyorai]], said to have been crafted from iron sand sourced from the [[:en:Shinano River|Shinano River]]. The hall was restored in [[:en:1954 CE|1954 CE]] to its original form.
== Cultural Properties == === National Important Cultural Properties === * Amida Hall - Designated under the former Ancient Shrines and Temples Preservation Act in [[:en:1906 CE|1906 CE]].
=== Niigata Prefecture-Designated Cultural Properties === * Large Gong - Crafted in [[:en:1437 CE|1437 CE]] with inscriptions by Saikata Yasaburō Mitsuyuki. * ''Daihannya-kyō'' (559 scrolls) - Donated in [[:en:1569 CE|1569 CE]].
=== Ojiya City-Designated Cultural Properties === * Mikoshi - A portable shrine featuring a phoenix on its roof, originally crafted in [[:en:1495 CE|1495 CE]] and restored in [[:en:1817 CE|1817 CE]]. * ''Uonuma Shrine Annual Event Record'' - Historical documents outlining shrine activities.
== Legends == === Uesugi Kenshin and the Divine Army === Uesugi Kenshin is said to have visited the shrine to pray for victory before crossing the [[:en:Shinano River|Shinano River]] en route to the [[:en:Kantō region|Kantō region]]. During one such visit, he reportedly saw a vision of [[Ame-no-Kaguyama]] and other deities marching toward Kantō. The vision inspired his troops and contributed to their subsequent victories.
=== The Rescued Soldier === During [[:en:Pacific War|Pacific War]], a soldier who had fallen into a dry well was rescued by a white-haired elderly man holding a lantern marked with "Kami-Yahiko Shrine." Upon being reunited with his unit, the elderly man disappeared.
=== The Giant Cedar === A massive cedar tree known as "Ōki-sama" stood on the shrine grounds until it was toppled by a storm. Its trunk reportedly stretched down the mountain, serving as a makeshift bridge.
== References == * Igarashi Shūtarō, ''Legends of Ojiya,'' Kōbunsha, 1985. * Ojiya City Historical Committee, ''Chronicle of Ojiya,'' Ojiya City Cultural Property Association, 1984. * Ojiya City Board of Education, ''History of Ojiya,'' 2007.
== External links == * [http://www.city.ojiya.niigata.jp/soshiki/shogai/uonumajinja-amidado.html Uonuma Shrine Amida Hall] - Ojiya City Official Website * [https://niigata-kankou.or.jp/spot/6089 Niigata Tourism Guide: Uonuma Shrine Amida Hall]

During the [[Sengoku period (Japan)|Sengoku period]], due to its shared deity with [[Yahiko Shrine]] in [[:en:Yahiko, Niigata|Yahiko]], it was referred to as "Kami-Yahiko Shrine." Historical documents from the era of [[:en:Uesugi Kenshin|Uesugi Kenshin]] mention it as "Kami-Yahiko Daimyōjin," and a plaque reading "Yahiko Daimyōjin" is displayed in the worship hall. The shrine served as a religious center for the region and was later called "Uonuma Shrine" during the [[:en:Bakumatsu|Bakumatsu]] period. It was relocated to its current location in Tsuchikawa from the earlier site at Kaminoyama.
Key historical milestones include: * [[Jōgan 3]] (861 CE): Uonuma Shrine received the Rank of [[Junior Fifth Rank|Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade]]. * [[Genkō 3]] (1333 CE): Shrine priests supported [[Nitta Yoshisada]] during his uprising. * [[Eikyō 9]] (1437 CE): The shrine received a large gong donated by Saikata Yasaburō Mitsuyuki (now a Niigata Prefecture-designated cultural property). * [[Eiroku 5]] (1562 CE): [[:en:Uesugi Kenshin|Uesugi Kenshin]] donated shrine lands and offerings for 18 branch shrines. * [[Eiroku 12]] (1569 CE): A hand-copied version of the ''[[Daihannya-kyō]]'' was donated to the shrine. * [[:en:Meiji 6|Meiji 6]] (1873 CE): Designated as a [[Gōsha]] and later promoted to [[Prefectural Shrine]].
== Shrine grounds == The main shrine gate is a [[Ryōbu torii]]. Adjacent to the purification fountain, stone steps lead to a historical water purification site. The shrine grounds include the Amida Hall to the left, the Kagura Hall to the right, and a monument commemorating the Russo-Japanese War nearby.
; Amida Hall The Amida Hall, constructed in [[:en:1563 CE|1563 CE]] during the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], is designated as a National Important Cultural Property. It is a modest hōgyō-zukuri (hipped roof) structure with thatched roofing. Inside are statues of [[:en:Amida Nyorai|Amida Nyorai]] and [[:en:Dainichi Nyorai|Dainichi Nyorai]], said to have been crafted from iron sand sourced from the [[:en:Shinano River|Shinano River]]. The hall was restored in [[:en:1954 CE|1954 CE]] to its original form.
== Cultural Properties == === National Important Cultural Properties === * Amida Hall - Designated under the former Ancient Shrines and Temples Preservation Act in [[:en:1906 CE|1906 CE]].
=== Niigata Prefecture-Designated Cultural Properties === * Large Gong - Crafted in [[:en:1437 CE|1437 CE]] with inscriptions by Saikata Yasaburō Mitsuyuki. * ''Daihannya-kyō'' (559 scrolls) - Donated in [[:en:1569 CE|1569 CE]].
=== Ojiya City-Designated Cultural Properties === * Mikoshi - A portable shrine featuring a phoenix on its roof, originally crafted in [[:en:1495 CE|1495 CE]] and restored in [[:en:1817 CE|1817 CE]]. * ''Uonuma Shrine Annual Event Record'' - Historical documents outlining shrine activities.
== Legends == === Uesugi Kenshin and the Divine Army === Uesugi Kenshin is said to have visited the shrine to pray for victory before crossing the [[:en:Shinano River|Shinano River]] en route to the [[:en:Kantō region|Kantō region]]. During one such visit, he reportedly saw a vision of [[Ame-no-Kaguyama]] and other deities marching toward Kantō. The vision inspired his troops and contributed to their subsequent victories.
=== The Rescued Soldier === During [[:en:Pacific War|Pacific War]], a soldier who had fallen into a dry well was rescued by a white-haired elderly man holding a lantern marked with "Kami-Yahiko Shrine." Upon being reunited with his unit, the elderly man disappeared.
=== The Giant Cedar === A massive cedar tree known as "Ōki-sama" stood on the shrine grounds until it was toppled by a storm. Its trunk reportedly stretched down the mountain, serving as a makeshift bridge.
== References == * Igarashi Shūtarō, ''Legends of Ojiya,'' Kōbunsha, 1985. * Ojiya City Historical Committee, ''Chronicle of Ojiya,'' Ojiya City Cultural Property Association, 1984. * Ojiya City Board of Education, ''History of Ojiya,'' 2007.
== External links == * [http://www.city.ojiya.niigata.jp/soshiki/shogai/uonumajinja-amidado.html Uonuma Shrine Amida Hall] - Ojiya City Official Website * [https://niigata-kankou.or.jp/spot/6089 Niigata Tourism Guide: Uonuma Shrine Amida Hall]

Inside are statues of [[:en:Amida Nyorai|Amida Nyorai]] and [[:en:Dainichi Nyorai|Dainichi Nyorai]], said to have been crafted from iron sand sourced from the [[:en:Shinano River|Shinano River]]. During one such visit, he reportedly saw a vision of [[Ame-no-Kaguyama]] and other deities marching toward Kantō. The vision inspired his troops and contributed to their subsequent victories.

=== Niigata Prefecture-Designated Cultural Properties === * Large Gong - Crafted in [[:en:1437 CE|1437 CE]] with inscriptions by Saikata Yasaburō Mitsuyuki. * ''Daihannya-kyō'' (559 scrolls) - Donated in [[:en:1569 CE|1569 CE]].
=== Ojiya City-Designated Cultural Properties === * Mikoshi - A portable shrine featuring a phoenix on its roof, originally crafted in [[:en:1495 CE|1495 CE]] and restored in [[:en:1817 CE|1817 CE]]. * ''Uonuma Shrine Annual Event Record'' - Historical documents outlining shrine activities.
== Legends == === Uesugi Kenshin and the Divine Army === Uesugi Kenshin is said to have visited the shrine to pray for victory before crossing the [[:en:Shinano River|Shinano River]] en route to the [[:en:Kantō region|Kantō region]]. During one such visit, he reportedly saw a vision of [[Ame-no-Kaguyama]] and other deities marching toward Kantō. The vision inspired his troops and contributed to their subsequent victories.
=== The Rescued Soldier === During [[:en:Pacific War|Pacific War]], a soldier who had fallen into a dry well was rescued by a white-haired elderly man holding a lantern marked with "Kami-Yahiko Shrine." Upon being reunited with his unit, the elderly man disappeared.
=== The Giant Cedar === A massive cedar tree known as "Ōki-sama" stood on the shrine grounds until it was toppled by a storm. Its trunk reportedly stretched down the mountain, serving as a makeshift bridge.
== References == * Igarashi Shūtarō, ''Legends of Ojiya,'' Kōbunsha, 1985. * Ojiya City Historical Committee, ''Chronicle of Ojiya,'' Ojiya City Cultural Property Association, 1984. * Ojiya City Board of Education, ''History of Ojiya,'' 2007.
== External links == * [http://www.city.ojiya.niigata.jp/soshiki/shogai/uonumajinja-amidado.html Uonuma Shrine Amida Hall] - Ojiya City Official Website * [https://niigata-kankou.or.jp/spot/6089 Niigata Tourism Guide: Uonuma Shrine Amida Hall]
